META.subject: Biology
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 2.2 (8 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Biology
UNIT.content:
• Overview of the plant transport system (xylem and phloem)
• Root structure and function — root hair cells and absorption of water and mineral salts
• Stem structure — external and internal features; arrangement of vascular bundles in herbaceous and woody stems
• Leaf structure — internal anatomy; role in transport and gaseous exchange
• Uptake and movement of water and mineral salts — osmosis, active transport, and the apoplast/symplast pathways
• Transpiration — definition, factors affecting the rate, and its significance to water movement (transpiration pull)
• Translocation — movement of manufactured food (sucrose and amino acids) through phloem; source-to-sink concept
• Adaptations of plants to different water availabilities (xerophytes, hydrophytes, mesophytes)
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) describe the structure and function of the plant transport system, including the roles of xylem and phloem,
b) explain how water and mineral salts are absorbed by roots and transported through the plant body,
c) investigate and explain the factors that affect the rate of transpiration and its significance in water movement,
d) explain the process of translocation of manufactured food substances from source to sink,
e) relate the structural adaptations of plant tissues to their transport functions,
f) appreciate the importance of plant transport processes to agriculture and food security in Kenya.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: The learner analyses why crops in Rift Valley farms wilt during dry spells and uses evidence from experiments (e.g., celery/white carnation dye experiments) to construct explanations about water movement in plants.
• Communication and Collaboration: The learner works in groups to design and present experiments on transpiration using a potometer, shares findings with peers, and co-constructs annotated diagrams of vascular tissue.
• Imagination and Creativity: The learner designs and draws cumulative models of the whole-plant transport system, revising them lesson by lesson as new evidence is gathered, and proposes creative solutions (e.g., irrigation scheduling) to reduce wilting in Kenyan farms.
• Learning to Learn: The learner reflects on growing understanding by updating the Driving Question Board and self-assessing model revisions across the unit.
• Self-efficacy: The learner develops confidence by independently designing a controlled transpiration investigation and presenting conclusions to the class.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: The learner handles laboratory specimens (real roots, stem cross-sections) and living plant material carefully and disposes of materials responsibly, reflecting stewardship of the natural environment.
• Respect: The learner appreciates diverse contributions during group discussions on how Kenyan farmers manage water for crops, and respects peers' experimental observations even when results differ.
• Unity: The learner collaborates across gender and ability groups when conducting transpiration experiments and constructing shared plant transport models.
• Social Justice: The learner connects the science of plant transport to issues of food security and equitable access to water for smallholder farmers in regions such as Turkana and the semi-arid Eastern Province.
UNIT.sep:
• Asking Questions and Defining Problems: Students generate investigable questions from the anchoring phenomenon (wilting maize crops) and refine them into a class Driving Question Board throughout the unit.
• Planning and Carrying Out Investigations: Students design controlled experiments to investigate factors affecting transpiration rate (light intensity, wind, humidity, leaf surface area) using improvised potometers built from locally available materials.
• Analysing and Interpreting Data: Students collect quantitative data on water uptake rates, construct graphs, and identify patterns linking transpiration conditions to water loss in plants.
• Developing and Using Models: Students iteratively build and revise a whole-plant transport diagram model — adding root absorption, xylem/phloem pathways, transpiration pull, and translocation — lesson by lesson.
• Constructing Explanations: Students use osmosis, active transport, cohesion-tension, and pressure-flow concepts to explain how water and sugars move against or with concentration gradients.
• Engaging in Argument from Evidence: Students use experimental data and microscopy observations to argue for or against proposed mechanisms of water movement (e.g., root pressure vs. transpiration pull).
• Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information: Students research the impact of drought on Kenyan staple crops (maize, beans), evaluate sources, and communicate findings through annotated diagrams and oral presentations.
UNIT.pcis:
• Environmental Conservation: The learner explores how deforestation around water catchment areas such as Mau Forest reduces transpiration rates and disrupts local water cycles, connecting plant transport science to conservation advocacy.
• Agriculture and Food Security: The learner directly connects the mechanisms of water and nutrient transport in plants to the productivity of staple Kenyan crops (maize, beans, sukuma wiki) and to irrigation practices used by farmers in Mwea and Galana-Kulalu.
• Climate Change: The learner investigates how rising temperatures and prolonged droughts (as experienced in northern Kenya) increase transpiration rates and accelerate wilting, linking plant biology to climate adaptation strategies.
• Health Education: The learner recognises that disruption of plant nutrient transport (e.g., mineral salt deficiencies) reduces the nutritional quality of food crops such as ugali (maize flour) and sukuma wiki, with direct implications for community nutrition.
• Safety and Security: The learner handles laboratory chemicals (iodine solution, eosin dye) and sharp tools (razor blades for stem sections) following proper safety protocols.
UNIT.careers:
• Botany / Plant Science: Understanding plant vascular systems is foundational for careers in plant research at institutions such as KEFRI (Kenya Forestry Research Institute) or KALRO (Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organisation).
• Agronomy and Agricultural Extension: Knowledge of water uptake, transpiration, and nutrient transport informs irrigation scheduling, fertiliser application, and crop management advice given to Kenyan smallholder farmers.
• Horticulture: Careers in Kenya's cut-flower industry (Naivasha) and fruit/vegetable export sector require understanding of post-harvest water relations and vascular tissue integrity to maintain produce quality.
• Medicine / Pharmacognosy: Many Kenyan medicinal plants are used in traditional and modern medicine; understanding phloem transport of secondary metabolites (alkaloids, tannins) is relevant to pharmaceutical research.
• Environmental Science and Conservation: Understanding transpiration and its role in the water cycle supports careers in watershed management, reforestation (e.g., Mau Forest restoration), and climate change mitigation.
• Food Technology: Knowledge of how mineral salts and sugars are translocated in plants informs biofortification programmes aimed at improving the nutritional content of staple crops such as maize and beans.
UNIT.focus: This unit focuses on how plants move water, mineral salts, and manufactured food substances through specialised vascular tissues (xylem and phloem), anchored in the real-world phenomenon of maize crops wilting on Kenyan farms during dry seasons. Students use a phenomenon-driven, inquiry-based approach — conducting hands-on experiments with local plant materials, building iterative models, and linking molecular-level processes (osmosis, active transport, cohesion-tension) to whole-plant and ecosystem-level outcomes. Throughout the unit, the science of plant transport is continuously connected to Kenyan agricultural contexts, food security, and environmental conservation, ensuring relevance and deep conceptual understanding.
UNIT.phenomenon:
ANCHORING PHENOMENON — "The Wilting Maize of Kibwezi":

A farmer in Kibwezi, Makueni County — a semi-arid region of Kenya — notices that her maize crop wilts dramatically every afternoon during the dry season, even though she watered the soil that same morning. By evening, some plants recover; others do not. She also notices that the maize plants growing near a dry seasonal riverbed wilt far sooner than those on the lower, wetter slopes. Meanwhile, a neighbour's sukuma wiki plot, which is shaded by a large mango tree, barely wilts at all. The farmer is puzzled: the soil has water, she can see it — so why are the plants wilting? Why do some recover but others die? Why does shade seem to protect the sukuma wiki?

This phenomenon is puzzling because it challenges students' intuitive belief that "watering = no wilting." It reveals that water availability in soil is not the same as water availability inside the plant, and that something must be actively moving water from soil through root, stem, and leaf — and losing it somewhere along the way. The scenario is directly familiar to Kenyan students, many of whom have observed wilting crops on family farms or school gardens, making it personally meaningful and emotionally engaging.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• The Coloured Carnation: A white carnation (or celery stalk) placed in red food-colouring solution turns red within hours — students observe coloured veins in the petals/leaves, raising the question of exactly which tissue carried the dye and how fast it travelled. (Supports: xylem structure and function, transpiration pull.)
• The Sweating Leaf Bag: A clear plastic bag tied around a leafy branch of a tree in the school compound accumulates visible water droplets within 30–60 minutes on a sunny Nairobi morning, even though no water was added. (Supports: transpiration, stomatal function, environmental factors affecting water loss.)
• The Girdled Tree: Students examine photographs of trees in Karura Forest whose bark has been ring-stripped by poachers; the trees survive briefly but their leaves eventually yellow and die from the roots upward. (Supports: phloem translocation — cutting the phloem starves the roots of sugars.)
• The Drought-Resistant Cactus vs. the Wilting Bean: Side-by-side images of a cactus thriving in Turkana's arid landscape and a bean seedling wilting in a dry pot prompt students to ask: what structural features allow some plants to survive with almost no water? (Supports: plant adaptations — xerophytes vs. mesophytes.)
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: How does water and food travel through an entire plant — from the soil to the highest leaf — and why does this journey sometimes fail, causing crops like maize in Kibwezi to wilt and die?

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. How are water and mineral salts transported in plants?
2. How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?
UNIT.storylineThread:
Lesson 1 — Encounter the Phenomenon (The Wilting Maize of Kibwezi): Students view photographs and a short description of the wilting maize scenario in Kibwezi. They share personal observations from home farms and school gardens, record initial ideas about why plants wilt, and co-create the first Driving Question Board (DQB) with "What We Think We Know" and "What We Wonder" columns. Outcome: Students generate investigable questions and commit to initial predictions.

Lesson 2 — What Do Plants Need to Survive? (Plant Needs and the Case for a Transport System): Students brainstorm what plants need (water, mineral salts, sunlight, carbon dioxide) and map where each resource enters the plant and where it is needed. They recognise that resources enter at one location but are used at another — establishing the logical necessity of a transport system. Students add new questions to the DQB. Outcome: Students articulate why an internal transport system is essential and identify it as the key to solving the wilting mystery.

Lesson 3 — The Big Picture: Overview of the Plant Transport System (Xylem and Phloem): Teacher introduces xylem and phloem as the two transport highways of the plant using a whole-plant diagram. Students label diagrams and use coloured arrows to show the direction of water/mineral salt movement (upward, xylem) vs. food movement (downward and upward, phloem). Students begin their cumulative model. Outcome: Students correctly identify the two vascular tissues and their transport directions.

Lesson 4 — The Entry Point: Root Structure and Water Absorption: Students observe real roots of bean or maize seedlings (grown in the school garden) under a hand lens and, where possible, under a microscope. They draw and label root hair cells, the cortex, endodermis, and xylem. They link the large surface area of root hairs to efficient water and mineral salt absorption. Students conduct a simple osmosis demonstration (potato strips in salt solution vs. water) to confirm the mechanism. Outcome: Students explain how water enters the root by osmosis and how mineral salts enter by active transport, and add these mechanisms to their cumulative model.

Lesson 5 — The Highway: Stem Structure and Vascular Bundles: Students cut cross-sections of herbaceous stems (e.g., sunflower or bean stems from the school garden) with a razor blade, stain with iodine or eosin, and observe vascular bundle arrangement. They compare herbaceous and woody stem diagrams. The coloured celery/carnation experiment is set up (results observed in Lesson 6). Students update their cumulative model to include stem pathways. Outcome: Students identify xylem and phloem within vascular bundles and describe their arrangement in herbaceous stems.

Lesson 6 — The Destination: Leaf Structure and Transpiration: Students dissect a leaf (e.g., sukuma wiki or a Ficus leaf), observe the waxy cuticle, veins, and stomate positions (lower epidermis impression using clear nail varnish). They observe and record results of the coloured dye experiment from Lesson 5 — confirming xylem transport. The "sweating leaf bag" demonstration (tied around a branch in the school compound) is reviewed. Students define transpiration and list factors that affect its rate. Outcome: Students explain how water is lost through stomata and why the rate varies, and add leaf anatomy and stomata to their cumulative model.

Lesson 7 — The Engine: Transpiration Pull, Cohesion-Tension, and Root Pressure: Students conduct a structured investigation using an improvised potometer (a cut stem, rubber tubing, capillary tube, and ruler) to measure water uptake under different conditions (fan blowing = wind, lamp = light, petroleum jelly on leaves = blocked stomata). They collect and graph data, then construct an explanation linking transpiration pull (cohesion-tension mechanism) and root pressure to upward water movement. Students return to the DQB and answer: "Why does the maize wilt even after watering?" — the rate of transpiration exceeds the rate of water absorption. Outcome: Students explain the cohesion-tension mechanism and use quantitative data to support it; they partially resolve the anchoring phenomenon.

Lesson 8 — The Return Journey: Translocation and Final Model Build: Students examine the "girdled tree" photographs and the concept of source-to-sink translocation through phloem (sucrose, amino acids). They use the pressure-flow hypothesis to explain translocation direction. Students then complete their final cumulative model of the entire plant transport system — annotating xylem (water + minerals, root to leaf, transpiration pull), phloem (sugars + amino acids, source to sink, pressure flow), osmosis at root hairs, and stomatal transpiration. Groups present their models and return to the original Kibwezi wilting scenario to construct a full evidence-based explanation. DQB is reviewed: all driving questions are answered. Outcome: Students produce a complete, annotated whole-plant transport model and write a scientific explanation of the anchoring phenomenon, demonstrating mastery of all unit learning outcomes.

LESSONS.title: The Wilting Maize of Kibwezi: Encountering the Phenomenon
LESSONS.aresKeywords: wilting, transport, xylem, phloem, water, vascular system, phenomenon, observation, prediction

slo.purpose: To launch curiosity about plant water transport by confronting students with a puzzling real-world phenomenon from Kibwezi, Makueni County, that challenges the intuitive belief that watering a plant is sufficient to prevent wilting
slo.knowledge: Students will recall prior knowledge about plant needs and begin to recognise that water availability in soil does not guarantee water availability inside the plant
slo.skills: Students will observe photographic evidence critically, share personal farm and garden experiences, record initial ideas, generate investigable questions, and co-construct the first Driving Question Board
slo.attitudes: Students will demonstrate curiosity, openness to uncertainty, and willingness to revise prior assumptions when confronted with puzzling evidence
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This opening lesson anchors the entire unit. It introduces the central phenomenon — the wilting maize of Kibwezi — that students will return to in every subsequent lesson. By surfacing prior ideas and generating genuine questions, students develop ownership of the investigation and a personal reason to understand plant transport.
slo.safetyNotes: No laboratory hazards in this lesson. If printed photographs are distributed, ensure edges are not sharp. Students sharing personal farm experiences should be handled sensitively as some families may have experienced crop failure and associated economic hardship.

LESSONS.overview: Students are introduced to the anchoring phenomenon through photographs and a written scenario describing a Kibwezi farmer whose maize wilts in the afternoon despite morning watering. Students share personal observations from home farms and school gardens, record initial explanatory ideas individually, and then co-create the class Driving Question Board (DQB) with two columns: What We Think We Know and What We Wonder. The lesson ends with students committing to written initial predictions that will be revisited throughout the unit.

framework.learnerExperience: Students sit in groups of four. Before seeing any photographs, each student receives a prompt card that reads: A farmer in Kibwezi waters her maize early in the morning. By midday, the leaves are drooping. By evening, some plants stand upright again. Others stay drooped and eventually die. The neighbouring sukuma wiki, growing in the shade of a large mango tree, looks healthy all day. Write down two things you think you already know about why plants wilt, and one question this story makes you want to ask. Students write independently for three minutes, then share within their group for two minutes. Groups do not report out yet — this preserves the diversity of ideas for the Observe phase.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute prompt cards and say: Read this story carefully. Think about any time you have seen a plant wilt — on your family shamba, at the school garden, anywhere. Write your own ideas first before talking to your group. [WAIT 3 MINUTES IN SILENCE — circulate and read over shoulders to identify the most common prior ideas and the most surprising questions to highlight later.] Then say: Now share what you wrote with your group. Listen carefully — do others agree with you, or do they have different ideas? You do not need to reach agreement yet. [WAIT 2 MINUTES.] Do not take whole-class feedback yet. Say: Hold your ideas. We are about to look at some photographs that will give us more to think about.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Elicit-before-tell: activating prior knowledge before presenting the phenomenon ensures students connect new observations to existing mental models, making subsequent cognitive conflict more productive. Individual writing before group talk protects minority ideas from being suppressed by dominant voices.
framework.formativeAssessment: Circulate and read student prompt cards during the silent writing phase. Look for: (1) students who write that wilting is caused by lack of water in soil — this is the target misconception to disrupt; (2) students who already mention heat or sun as a cause — affirm this as an important observation to investigate; (3) students who write questions about the inside of the plant — note these students to invite them to share during the DQB phase.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher projects or pins up three photographs: (A) a close-up of wilted maize leaves curling inward in a dry Kibwezi field under bright midday sun; (B) a wider shot showing maize near a dry seasonal riverbed wilting more severely than maize on a lower, wetter slope; (C) lush, upright sukuma wiki growing under the canopy shade of a large mango tree, with visibly moist-looking soil around the maize in photograph A indicating the farmer did indeed water that morning. Students observe each photograph for 60 seconds silently, then annotate a printed observation sheet with three columns: What I See, What It Makes Me Think, What It Makes Me Wonder. After individual annotation, groups share observations for three minutes.
framework.teacherMoves: Display photograph A and say: Look carefully at this image. Do not explain it yet — just observe. What do you actually see? [WAIT 60 SECONDS IN SILENCE.] Then say: Now write in the first column — What I See. Only things you can actually see in the photograph, not your explanations yet. [WAIT 90 SECONDS.] Display photograph B and repeat. Then photograph C. After all three photographs, say: Now look at your What It Makes Me Think column. Here is a challenge — the soil in that photograph is damp. The farmer watered that morning. If the soil has water in it, why might the plant still be wilting? Does your original prediction still feel right? [WAIT 2 MINUTES for writing.] Then invite groups to share one surprising observation each. Use the phrase: What did you notice that surprised your group? Avoid evaluating responses — say: That is an interesting observation — let us keep it in mind as we build our questions.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured observation protocol (See-Think-Wonder) prevents students from jumping to premature explanations. The deliberate cognitive conflict — showing damp soil alongside wilted plants — is the key disruptive moment that makes the phenomenon puzzling and motivates the need for a transport system. Separating observation from explanation builds scientific practice habits.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect observation sheets at the end of the lesson or photograph them. Check whether students distinguish between observations and explanations — a key scientific skill. Listen during group sharing for the phrase the soil has water so why is it wilting — students who articulate this tension are ready to generate the most productive DQB questions. Students who still say it is wilting because there is no water need targeted questioning in subsequent lessons.

framework.learnerExperience: Students attempt to explain the phenomenon using only what they currently know. Each group receives a large sheet of paper and draws a simple outline of a maize plant — roots, stem, leaves. They use arrows and labels to show what they think is happening to water on a hot afternoon in Kibwezi. Groups post their diagrams on the classroom wall. The class does a gallery walk (3 minutes) reading other groups diagrams silently before sitting down. Students then write an individual first draft explanation (3 sentences maximum) in their science notebooks answering the question: Why might a maize plant wilt even when the soil around it has water?
framework.teacherMoves: Say: You have observed the photographs and shared your ideas. Now I want each group to draw what you think is happening inside and outside the maize plant on a hot Kibwezi afternoon. Use arrows. Label what is moving and where it is going. You have four minutes. [WAIT 4 MINUTES — circulate and ask probing questions such as: Where does the water go after it enters the root? What happens to water in the leaves? Do not give answers.] After the gallery walk say: Look at the diagrams on the wall. Do they all agree? What is different between them? [WAIT 1 MINUTE.] Then say: Now write your own personal explanation — three sentences, in your notebook. Begin with the words: I think the maize wilts because... This is your prediction for the whole unit. We will come back to it at the end.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Initial model drawing externalises mental models, making tacit ideas visible to both teacher and student. The gallery walk creates low-stakes peer review and exposes students to alternative ideas without competitive evaluation. The individual written prediction creates a personal intellectual stake in the unit — students want to find out if they were right.
framework.formativeAssessment: Photograph all group diagrams before the gallery walk is complete. Look for: (1) diagrams that show water stopping at the root — indicates gap in understanding of whole-plant transport; (2) diagrams that show water leaving the leaf — indicates readiness for transpiration concepts in Lessons 6 and 7; (3) diagrams that include the sun or heat as a variable — affirm as scientifically important. Individual notebook predictions serve as a pre-assessment benchmark to compare with post-unit explanations.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher reveals the unit Driving Question printed on a large card and posts it at the top of a dedicated DQB display: How does water and food travel through an entire plant — from the soil to the highest leaf — and why does this journey sometimes fail, causing crops like maize in Kibwezi to wilt and die? Each student receives three sticky notes. On the first sticky note they write one thing they think they know. On the second they write one question they genuinely wonder about. On the third they write what they think the most important thing to find out is. Students post their sticky notes in the correct columns of the DQB: What We Think We Know and What We Wonder. The teacher reads a selection aloud and clusters similar questions, inviting the class to suggest category names for the clusters.
framework.teacherMoves: Post the driving question card and read it aloud. Say: This is the big question we will be investigating for the next several weeks. Every lesson will help us answer a part of it. Right now, we are going to fill our Driving Question Board — this is where we collect all our ideas and questions. It will grow every lesson. [Distribute sticky notes.] Say: On your first sticky note, write one thing you are fairly confident you already know about how plants get water. On the second, write a genuine question — something you really want to find out. On the third, write what you think is the single most important thing we need to understand to explain the Kibwezi wilting mystery. [WAIT 3 MINUTES IN SILENCE for writing.] Then invite students to come up and post notes in the correct columns. Read five or six aloud, clustering as you go. Say: I notice several of you are asking about what happens to water inside the stem — let us call that cluster The Inside Journey. Who else has a question that belongs there? Affirm all questions. Say: Every question on this board is a good scientific question. Our job this term is to answer all of them.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB is a living artefact of the class intellectual community. Clustering questions gives students agency in organising the inquiry. Naming clusters using student language rather than textbook language maintains authenticity. Returning to the DQB in every subsequent lesson creates coherence across the unit and signals that the teacher values student-generated questions as the engine of learning.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all sticky notes after class. Sort questions into three categories: (1) questions that will be directly answered in the unit — use these to inform lesson emphasis; (2) questions that reveal key misconceptions — e.g., Do plants drink like animals? or Does water go up the plant by itself? — address these explicitly in Lessons 4 and 7; (3) surprisingly sophisticated questions — e.g., How does water get to the very top of a tall tree? — celebrate these publicly in the next lesson to reward intellectual risk-taking.

framework.learnerExperience: Students begin their cumulative unit model — a whole-plant diagram that they will add to in every lesson. Each student draws a simple outline of a maize plant in their science notebook (roots, stem with at least one vascular bundle indicated by a vertical line, and leaves with stomata indicated as dots on the lower surface). They label only what they currently know with confidence, and mark with a question mark any part they are unsure about. At the bottom of the model page, they write today the model shows what I already thought was true. By the end of this unit, I expect this model to show... and complete the sentence. The teacher explains that this model will be updated at the end of every lesson as their understanding grows.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: Scientists do not just ask questions — they also build models. A model is a drawing or diagram that shows how you think something works. We are going to build a model of the whole plant transport system across this entire unit. Today we start with what we know, or think we know. Draw a maize plant that fills a full page of your notebook — roots at the bottom, stem in the middle, leaves at the top. Label any part you are confident about. Put a question mark next to anything you are unsure of. [WAIT 4 MINUTES.] Then say: At the bottom of your model, write: Today my model shows... and describe in one sentence what your model currently represents. Then write: By the end of this unit, I expect my model to show... Give yourself a challenge. [WAIT 2 MINUTES.] Collect one example to photograph as a baseline. Say: We will return to this model at the end of every single lesson and add new understanding. By Lesson 8, your model should explain everything the Kibwezi farmer is experiencing.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative model building is the unit-long sense-making spine. Starting the model in Lesson 1 — even when students know very little — creates a concrete artefact that makes learning visible over time. Question marks on the model legitimise not knowing and reframe uncertainty as the beginning of investigation rather than a failure of knowledge. The student-authored prediction sentence creates intellectual investment in the completed model.
framework.formativeAssessment: Photograph or collect baseline models before students leave. Do not grade them. Use them to: (1) identify students who already show water moving through roots, stem, and leaves — they may be ready for extension tasks in later lessons; (2) identify students who draw water only in the soil or only in the roots — these students need targeted scaffolding in Lesson 4; (3) note whether students place stomata — few will, but those who do show readiness for Lesson 6 content. Return models to students at the start of Lesson 2 for updating.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After this lesson, consider: Did all students genuinely engage with the puzzle, or did some dismiss it because they thought they already knew the answer? Were the photographs vivid enough to make the Kibwezi scenario feel real and personally relevant to students whose families farm? Did the DQB generate authentic questions from students, or were students writing what they thought the teacher wanted to see? Were there questions posted that surprised you — questions you had not anticipated? How will you use those questions to shape subsequent lessons? Did any student share a personal story about crop failure or wilting that could be honoured and woven into future lessons as a local data point? Reflect on wait time: did you give students full silence during the three-minute individual writing periods, or did you fill the silence with prompts? Extended silence is uncomfortable but essential for genuine thinking. Finally, note any student who expressed scepticism — saying for example that this is obvious or of course it wilts in the sun. These students need deeper cognitive challenge; the potometer investigation in Lesson 7 is likely to be the moment that most productively disrupts their certainty.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: We observed photographs of wilted maize crops in Kibwezi, Makueni County, where the soil was visibly damp from morning watering but the plants were still drooping by midday. We noticed that maize near the dry seasonal riverbed wilted sooner, while sukuma wiki growing in the shade of a mango tree stayed upright and healthy all day.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: We learned that our first instinct — that wilting means the soil has no water — is challenged by this scenario. The soil had water, yet the plants still wilted. This tells us that there must be something happening inside the plant that we do not yet fully understand. We also learned that shade, slope, and soil moisture all seem to affect how badly a plant wilts.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: We cannot fully explain the wilting yet. We have recorded our first predictions and initial ideas, but we know these may be incomplete or incorrect. Our Driving Question Board shows the questions we need to investigate. By the end of this unit, we aim to construct a complete scientific explanation of what the Kibwezi farmer is observing — one that is supported by evidence from our investigations.

LESSONS.title: What Do Plants Need to Survive? — Mapping the Case for a Transport System

slo.purpose: To establish that plants require multiple resources that enter at different locations from where they are used, creating the logical necessity for an internal transport system — the key to explaining why the Kibwezi maize wilts.
slo.knowledge: Students identify the essential needs of a plant (water, mineral salts, carbon dioxide, sunlight/light energy) and map the specific location where each resource enters the plant and the location(s) where each resource is needed or used.
slo.skills: Students construct a resource-flow diagram (annotated plant outline) showing entry points and use points for each plant resource, and use this diagram to articulate — in their own words — why a transport system must exist inside the plant.
slo.attitudes: Students appreciate that scientific reasoning involves identifying gaps between what we observe (water in soil) and what we need to explain (water in the leaf), and value the practice of following evidence to build logical arguments.
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants? How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1, students observed the wilting maize phenomenon and generated initial questions. In Lesson 2, students zoom out to ask: what does a plant actually need, and where do those needs come from? By mapping resource entry points against resource use points, students discover the central tension — resources enter at one place but are consumed at another — and conclude that something inside the plant must be doing the moving. This conclusion becomes the engine that drives the rest of the evidence-gathering sequence.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. If students handle live plant specimens (maize seedling, sukuma wiki leaf), remind them to wash hands after handling soil or plant material. Ensure no student has a known plant allergy before passing specimens around.

LESSONS.overview: Students begin by revisiting the Kibwezi wilting maize scenario from Lesson 1 and the questions captured on the Driving Question Board (DQB). Through a structured brainstorm, they list everything a plant needs to survive, then use a large annotated diagram of a maize plant to physically map where each resource enters versus where it is used. The mismatch between entry point and use point creates a productive tension: if water enters at the root and is needed at the leaf, something must be carrying it. Students name this the transport system and connect it directly to the wilting mystery. New questions generated are added to the DQB. The lesson closes with students revising their initial mental model of the plant to include an internal pathway.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown a large projected image of the Kibwezi farmer's wilting maize field alongside a healthy sukuma wiki plot shaded by a mango tree (same images from Lesson 1). Individually, students write a response in their science journals to the prompt: 'List everything you think a plant needs to stay alive and healthy. Next to each item, write WHERE the plant gets it from.' Students then share with a shoulder partner before a class discussion is opened.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two images silently for 30 seconds before speaking. Say: 'Before we talk, I want you to think like a farmer — like this woman in Kibwezi. What does her maize plant actually need to survive?' Issue the journal prompt on the board. Allow 4 minutes of silent individual writing. After 4 minutes, say: 'Turn to your partner and share your list. You have 2 minutes — each person must speak.' After pair-share, cold-call 5 students (aim for gender balance and include at least one student who may have farm experience): 'Achieng, what is one thing on your list that your partner did NOT have?' Record all responses verbatim on the board under the heading: WHAT PLANTS NEED. Do not correct or affirm yet — simply record. Apply 10-second wait time after each response before moving to the next student.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Activating Prior Knowledge via Journal Prompt + Think-Pair-Share. This strategy surfaces students' existing mental models of plant needs before instruction, giving the teacher a formative baseline and giving students ownership of the starting point. Recording all answers without judgment models scientific openness and prevents early closure.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans journal entries during pair-share. Observable indicator: student lists include at least 3 of the 4 key plant needs (water, mineral salts/nutrients, sunlight, carbon dioxide). If fewer than half the class lists mineral salts/nutrients, flag this gap and address it explicitly in Phase 2. Listen for misconceptions such as 'plants get food from soil' — note these for targeted correction in the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of four receive: (a) a large outline drawing of a maize plant (roots, stem, leaves, with a soil line drawn) printed on A3 paper, (b) a set of 12 colour-coded resource cards (three cards each for: WATER, MINERAL SALTS, CARBON DIOXIDE, SUNLIGHT/LIGHT ENERGY — each card states the resource name and one clue sentence), and (c) a resource reference sheet with four short factual paragraphs (one per resource) describing what it is, where the plant obtains it, and what the plant uses it for. Students read the reference sheet, then physically place each resource card on the diagram — green star stickers mark WHERE it enters, red arrow stickers mark WHERE it is used. Groups record observations in a two-column table: ENTRY POINT | USE POINT.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute materials and say: 'You have 15 minutes. Your job is to be plant detectives. Read the reference sheet carefully — it has real science in it — then decide together where to place each card on your maize diagram. Use the green stickers for entry, red arrows for use. Every group member must be able to explain every card placement.' Circulate continuously. Stop at each group for 90 seconds. Ask probing questions: 'You placed water entering at the root — where does your diagram show it going next? Show me with your finger.' and 'Your group placed sunlight entering at the leaf — but where exactly on the leaf? Does it enter underground leaves too?' After 15 minutes, call time. Say: 'Hold up your diagrams.' Do a gallery scan — look for groups that have placed mineral salts entering at the leaf (a common misconception) and flag those groups for discussion. Select two contrasting group diagrams (one strong, one with a misconception) to project under the visualiser or photograph and display.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Evidence Mapping on an Annotated Diagram. By physically placing resource cards on a plant outline, students make the abstract concept of resource location concrete and spatial. The two-column Entry Point vs Use Point table forces students to notice that entry and use are often NOT the same location — this productive tension is the core intellectual move of the lesson.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher observes card placements during circulation. Observable indicator of understanding: water and mineral salts are placed entering at roots (below soil line); carbon dioxide is placed entering at leaves (stomata); sunlight/light energy is placed being absorbed at leaves. Observable indicator of misconception: mineral salts placed at leaves, or 'food' placed entering from the soil. Teacher notes which groups need targeted questioning in Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Whole class examines two projected group diagrams. Students identify where the entry point and use point are the same location and where they are different. The teacher introduces the key concept: when a resource enters at one location but is needed at another, something must transport it. Students complete a structured reasoning frame in their journals: 'Water enters the maize plant at the ________ (location). Water is needed at the ________ (location). Because these are different, the plant must have a ________ to move water from one place to the other. Without this system, the leaf would ________, which is exactly what we saw in ________.' Students then apply the same logic to mineral salts and to sugars manufactured in the leaf (teacher introduces: leaves make sugar using sunlight and CO₂ — where must that sugar travel to feed the root?). Class discusses: what happens to the Kibwezi maize if this transport system breaks down or is overwhelmed?
framework.teacherMoves: Project the two group diagrams side by side. Say: 'I want everyone to look at these two diagrams and identify one place where the entry point and the use point are NOT the same location. You have 10 seconds to find one.' [Wait 10 seconds in silence.] Cold-call 3 students: 'Kamau — tell me one mismatch you see.' After three responses, say: 'So we have a problem. Water is down here [point to root] but the leaf needs it up here [point to leaf]. Carbon is in the air up here, but the root needs energy down there. How does any of it travel?' [Wait 12 seconds.] Accept 2–3 responses, then say: 'Scientists call this the transport system. And this is exactly what the Kibwezi farmer's maize depends on every single afternoon.' Distribute the reasoning frame. Allow 6 minutes for silent individual completion. Cold-call 2 students to read their completed frames aloud. Correct gently: 'Almost — the leaf makes sugar, not food from the soil. Let's adjust that.' Pose the extension question: 'If the maize plant is losing water out of its leaves faster than the transport system can deliver it from the root, what happens?' Wait 15 seconds. Cold-call 1 student.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Reasoning Frame (Claim–Evidence–Reasoning scaffold). The fill-in frame forces students to connect the spatial mismatch they observed on the diagram to the logical conclusion that a transport system must exist, and then explicitly link that conclusion back to the anchoring phenomenon. This is an NGSS Science and Engineering Practice: Constructing Explanations from Evidence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect completed reasoning frames at the end of the phase (or photograph them). Observable indicator of proficiency: student correctly names roots as water entry point, leaves as water use point, names 'transport system' as the connector, and uses 'wilt' or equivalent as the consequence. Observable indicator of developing understanding: student identifies entry and use points correctly but cannot yet articulate the consequence for the Kibwezi maize. Use this data to group students for Lesson 3 peer support.

framework.learnerExperience: Each student receives two sticky notes. On the first (blue), they write one NEW question today's activity has raised about how the transport system works — something they do not yet know. On the second (orange), they write one thing they now understand about the Kibwezi wilting maize that they did NOT understand at the start of Lesson 1. Students post both notes on the class DQB under the appropriate columns: NEW QUESTIONS | GROWING UNDERSTANDING. The teacher reads selected notes aloud and clusters the new questions by theme (e.g., 'How exactly does water travel up?', 'What carries the sugar?', 'Why do some plants wilt more than others?'), previewing upcoming lessons.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes. Say: 'You have 3 minutes — silence please. Blue note: one question you now have that you did not have before. Orange note: one thing you can now explain about Kibwezi that you could not explain on day one.' After 3 minutes, direct students to post notes on the DQB. Read 4 blue notes aloud — choose one about water movement, one about sugar, one about wilting, one unexpected. Say: 'These are the exact questions scientists asked when they first tried to understand plant transport. We are going to answer every single one of these over the next six lessons.' Point to specific new questions: 'This question — about how water moves up against gravity — that is Lesson 3. This one about what happens inside the stem — that is Lesson 4.' This explicit roadmapping builds anticipation and shows students their questions matter.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board — Metacognitive Tracking. The two-colour sticky note system separates new uncertainty (blue) from consolidated understanding (orange), making students' growing knowledge visible to themselves and to the class. Clustering new questions by the teacher models scientific categorisation and previews the lesson sequence as a coherent investigation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all orange notes before the next lesson. Observable indicator: student can state at least one specific causal link (e.g., 'Water enters at the root and must travel to the leaf, so the plant needs a transport system'). If orange notes are vague ('I learned about plants'), teacher plans a brief whole-class re-voicing at the start of Lesson 3 to sharpen understanding before moving on.

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the annotated maize plant diagram from Phase 2 and revise it to become their first cumulative transport model. They add: (1) a bold upward arrow along the stem labelled WATER + MINERAL SALTS PATHWAY (use blue pencil/pen), (2) a bold downward arrow along the stem labelled SUGAR PATHWAY (use red pencil/pen), (3) a label at the leaf: WATER LOST HERE → WILTING IF TOO FAST, and (4) a legend. Under the diagram, students write one sentence: 'This model helps explain the Kibwezi wilting mystery because…' and complete it. Students do NOT yet label the specific tissues (xylem/phloem) — those come in Lesson 3. The model is kept in their science journal as a running record.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Your maize diagram is about to become your first scientific model of plant transport. A model is not a drawing — it is an explanation. Every arrow, every label must mean something.' Demonstrate briefly on the projected blank diagram: draw one blue upward arrow and say, 'This arrow is not decoration. It represents real water molecules moving from this root cell, up through this stem, all the way to this leaf — right now, in every maize plant in Kibwezi.' Allow 8 minutes for model revision. Circulate and ask: 'If I removed your blue arrow from this diagram, what would happen to the maize in Kibwezi?' [Wait 10 seconds for student response.] Before closing, cold-call 2 students to read their sentence aloud. Affirm specificity: 'Excellent — you named the root and the leaf. That is precise scientific language.' Collect or photograph all models. Return at start of Lesson 3.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (NGSS Science and Engineering Practice: Developing and Using Models). By building the model in stages across lessons, students see science as a progressive, evidence-driven process rather than a fixed body of facts. The colour-coded pathway system (blue = water/minerals up; red = sugar down) builds the conceptual scaffold for xylem and phloem nomenclature in Lesson 3, reducing cognitive load.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reviews model diagrams and completion sentences before Lesson 3. Observable indicators of proficiency: diagram shows directionally correct pathways (water/minerals upward, sugar downward), water loss is labelled at the leaf, and the completion sentence explicitly connects the transport system to wilting. Observable gap: students who draw only one pathway (e.g., only water) need targeted questioning at the start of Lesson 3 about what the leaf produces and where it must send it.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did students successfully identify all four plant needs during Phase 1, or did mineral salts/nutrients remain invisible to most students? If mineral salts were absent from most lists, consider adding a short reading about Kenyan farmers using fertiliser (e.g., DAP fertiliser on maize in Rift Valley) at the start of Lesson 3 to cement this resource. (2) In Phase 2, how many groups correctly placed mineral salts entering at the root rather than the leaf? Groups that placed it at the leaf hold a persistent misconception (conflating 'nutrients sprayed on leaves' with normal mineral uptake) — plan a targeted 3-minute correction at the start of Lesson 3 using a soil-and-root photograph. (3) Were students able to articulate the 'entry point ≠ use point' tension independently, or did it only emerge after teacher prompting? If prompting was needed, the Phase 3 reasoning frame may need a partially-worked example added. (4) Review the DQB blue (new question) notes — do students' questions cluster around HOW water moves, or around WHY wilting happens? Questions about mechanism (how) signal readiness for Lesson 3 (xylem structure). Questions still anchored at the phenomenon level (why wilt) suggest students need one more phenomenon-grounding experience before moving to tissue-level explanation. Adjust Lesson 3 opening accordingly.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed (via annotated plant diagrams and resource cards) that different plant resources — water, mineral salts, carbon dioxide, and sunlight — enter the maize plant at different locations: water and mineral salts at the roots below the soil, carbon dioxide and sunlight at the leaves above the soil.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that because plant resources enter at one location but are needed and used at a different location (e.g., water enters at the root but is needed at the leaf), a plant must have an internal transport system to move resources from entry point to use point — and that without this system, the leaf cannot get the water it needs and wilts, exactly as seen in the Kibwezi maize field.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students can now begin to explain part of the Kibwezi wilting mystery: the maize plant wilts when water cannot reach the leaf fast enough through its transport system, even if water is present in the soil. The farmer's observation that 'the soil has water but the plant still wilts' makes sense because soil water and leaf water are separated by the entire length of the plant — and something must carry that water across that distance.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: plant transport system, plant needs, water uptake, mineral salts, carbon dioxide, sunlight, root entry point, leaf use point, wilting maize, Kibwezi, resource flow diagram, annotated plant model, entry point vs use point, driving question board, xylem phloem preview, sukuma wiki, maize, semi-arid Kenya, transport necessity, NGSS constructing explanations, NGSS developing models

LESSONS.title: The Big Picture: Overview of the Plant Transport System (Xylem and Phloem)

slo.purpose: To introduce students to the two vascular transport tissues — xylem and phloem — and establish the directional flow of water/mineral salts and manufactured food substances through the whole plant, directly connecting these transport highways to the wilting maize phenomenon in Kibwezi.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) identify xylem and phloem as the two vascular tissues responsible for transport in plants; (b) describe the direction of movement of water and mineral salts through xylem (soil → root → stem → leaf, upward); (c) describe the direction of movement of manufactured food substances through phloem (leaf → stem → root and other sinks, bidirectional but primarily downward); (d) locate xylem and phloem within a whole-plant diagram.
slo.skills: Labelling biological diagrams accurately; drawing and interpreting directional arrows to represent transport pathways; constructing an initial cumulative model of the plant transport system; comparing two transport systems using a graphic organiser; collaborative discussion and peer explanation.
slo.attitudes: Appreciation of the elegant design of the plant vascular system; curiosity about how microscopic tissues explain observable phenomena such as wilting; responsibility in contributing to group diagram work; respect for peers' emerging ideas during model construction.
slo.keyInquiry: 1. How are water and mineral salts transported in plants? 2. How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1 students anchored on the mystery of the Kibwezi wilting maize and generated initial predictions. In Lesson 2 they gathered evidence about osmosis and water potential as the driving force for water entry into roots. Now, in Lesson 3, students zoom out to the whole-plant scale: they must understand WHAT the transport highways are (xylem and phloem), WHERE they run, and in WHICH DIRECTION each fluid moves — because without this 'road map', they cannot yet explain why water absorbed by roots in the morning fails to reach the leaves before afternoon wilting occurs. This lesson lays the structural scaffold upon which Lessons 4–8 will hang the mechanisms of transpiration pull, translocation, and stomatal control.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. Students handle printed diagrams and coloured pencils/markers only. Ensure classroom movement during group work is orderly to prevent trips or falls. If a digital projector is used, avoid directing the beam at students' eyes.

LESSONS.overview: Students begin by briefly revisiting their Lesson 1 predictions and Lesson 2 evidence about osmosis, then pivot to the whole-plant scale. The teacher uses a large annotated diagram of a maize plant (rooted in Kibwezi soil) to introduce xylem and phloem as two distinct vascular 'highways'. Through a guided diagram-labelling activity, students use blue arrows for xylem flow and red arrows for phloem flow, building a directional map of the plant. A structured compare-and-contrast graphic organiser (Venn diagram) consolidates differences between xylem and phloem. Students then produce the first version of their cumulative whole-plant transport model, placing it in their science notebooks for revision in later lessons. The lesson closes by reconnecting to the Kibwezi farmer's puzzle: now that students know the two highways exist, the driving question sharpens — what force pushes water UP through xylem against gravity all the way to the topmost maize leaf?

framework.learnerExperience: Students individually read a short two-sentence scenario card placed on their desks: 'The Kibwezi farmer cut a cross-section of a wilting maize stem. She saw two different kinds of tubes inside. One set of tubes was carrying water upward; another set seemed to carry something else in a different direction.' Students write a 2-minute silent prediction in their notebooks answering: (a) What do you think the two types of tubes are called? (b) Which direction does each fluid travel? (c) How might a blockage in one of these tubes explain the maize wilting even after morning watering? Students then share predictions with a shoulder partner (1 minute). Teacher cold-calls 4 students — two from different ability groups — to share predictions aloud. Teacher records key vocabulary that students already possess (e.g., 'veins', 'pipes', 'channels') on the Driving Question Board without correcting misconceptions yet.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute scenario cards as students settle. Say: 'Read the card silently — you have 90 seconds. Then write your three predictions. Do NOT look anything up yet; I want YOUR thinking.' [Wait 2 minutes.] 'Now share with your shoulder partner — one person speaks, one listens, then switch. You have 60 seconds each.' [Wait 2 minutes.] Cold-call: 'Akinyi, what did you predict the two tubes are called?' [Wait 10 seconds.] 'Omondi, do you agree with Akinyi, or did you predict something different?' [Wait 10 seconds.] 'Wanjiku, what direction did you think the water moves — and why?' [Wait 12 seconds.] 'Kamau, how did you connect a blocked tube to the wilting maize?' [Wait 15 seconds.] Record student language on the DQB under 'What we THINK we know'. Say: 'Hold onto these predictions — by the end of today you will be able to check them against real biology.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Prediction-with-Justification. Students are required not just to guess but to give a reason, activating prior schema from Lesson 2 (osmosis, water movement) and everyday observations of plant 'veins'. This surfaces misconceptions (e.g., 'all tubes carry the same thing upward') that the lesson will directly address, and creates cognitive dissonance that motivates learning.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Student notebooks show three written predictions with at least one justification linked to the wilting phenomenon. Teacher circulates during partner share and notes (on a clipboard checklist) which students correctly anticipate directional flow vs. those who believe water moves in all directions through one system — this informs cold-call selection and targeted questioning during Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Activity 2A — Whole-Plant Diagram Annotation (25 min): Each student receives a large printed black-and-white diagram of a whole maize plant (roots in labelled Kibwezi soil, stem, leaves, developing maize cob). The diagram includes callout boxes for: root hair zone, root cortex, stem cross-section, leaf cross-section, and leaf surface with stomata. Using the teacher's projected annotated master diagram and a two-page reading excerpt (provided on the ARES platform, adapted from the KICD Grade 10 Biology textbook), students: (1) label xylem and phloem in each callout box; (2) draw BLUE arrows showing the direction water and mineral salts travel through xylem (upward from root tip to leaf); (3) draw RED arrows showing the direction manufactured food (sugars) travel through phloem (from leaf downward to roots and also to the developing maize cob as a sink); (4) annotate in their own words what each tissue carries. Activity 2B — Structured Reading & Highlight (10 min): Students read the excerpt independently, highlighting in BLUE every sentence that describes xylem structure or function, and in YELLOW every sentence about phloem. They write one 'surprising fact' in the margin.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute diagram sheets and reading excerpts simultaneously. Project the master annotated diagram on the board — but cover the labels with sticky notes initially. Say: 'Before I reveal the labels, use the reading to find the answers yourself. You have 10 minutes to label your diagram and draw your arrows. I will circulate — I am looking for correct arrow DIRECTION, not perfect art.' [Circulate for 8 minutes, noting errors without correcting immediately. Make specific targeted comments: 'Fatuma, your blue arrow is going the right direction — now check where xylem sits in the stem cross-section callout box.' 'Baraka, you've drawn the red arrow going only downward — re-read paragraph three carefully.'] After 10 minutes: 'Let's check together.' Reveal one callout box at a time on the projected diagram. Say: 'Look at the root hair callout — who has the label in the same position as mine? Raise your hand.' [Count hands.] 'If you had it differently, correct it now in a different colour so you can see your own thinking shift.' Repeat for each callout box. For phloem direction: 'I want to challenge you — raise your hand if you drew the red arrow going BOTH toward the root AND toward the maize cob.' [Wait 10 seconds.] 'That is correct — this is called bidirectional movement. We will explain WHY next lesson, but for now mark it on your diagram.' After Activity 2B: Cold-call 3 students: 'Njeri, read me your surprising fact.' [Wait 10 seconds each.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Annotated Diagram with Colour-Coded Evidence Trails. Using two distinct colours (blue/red) forces students to mentally separate two transport systems that share the same stem — a common point of confusion. Annotation in students' own words (not copied text) requires processing, not transcription. The progressive reveal of the master diagram provides immediate feedback without removing the productive struggle of independent labelling.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Diagram shows correctly placed labels for xylem and phloem in ALL four callout boxes (root, stem, leaf cross-section, leaf surface). (2) Blue arrows are strictly unidirectional upward; red arrows are bidirectional with one arrow toward root AND one toward the maize cob. (3) Reading highlights are colour-coded correctly. Teacher collects 6 diagrams (two high, two mid, two emerging students identified from Lesson 2 data) at the end of this phase for rapid review before Phase 3 begins.

framework.learnerExperience: Activity 3A — Venn Diagram Compare & Contrast (12 min): In pairs, students complete a printed Venn diagram comparing xylem and phloem across six criteria: (i) type of cells (living/non-living); (ii) what is transported; (iii) direction of transport; (iv) energy requirement (passive/active); (v) location in the stem (relative position); (vi) connection to the Kibwezi wilting problem. Students use their annotated diagrams and the reading excerpt as reference. Activity 3B — Jigsaw Share-Out (8 min): Each pair joins another pair to form a group of four. Each pair takes one system (xylem OR phloem) and 'teaches' it to the other pair using only their Venn diagram — no reading allowed. The listening pair asks at least one question. Activity 3C — Phenomenon Reconnect Discussion (5 min): Teacher leads a whole-class discussion tying today's evidence to the Kibwezi farmer. Teacher poses: 'Now that you know the two transport highways — if the xylem of a maize plant becomes blocked or cannot pull water fast enough, what would happen to the leaf?' Students respond and teacher links their answers to the afternoon wilting pattern.
framework.teacherMoves: For Activity 3A: 'You and your partner have 12 minutes to fill the Venn diagram. The hardest row is row four — energy requirement. The reading excerpt gives you a clue in paragraph five. Find it.' [Circulate. Targeted prompt if stuck: 'Think about what you know about osmosis from Lesson 2 — does osmosis need energy?'] For Activity 3B: 'When you teach your system, you must tell your partners: WHAT it carries, WHICH direction, and HOW that relates to the wilting maize. You have 4 minutes each.' [Monitor groups. If a group finishes early: 'Add a seventh row to your Venn diagram — what would happen to the plant if ONLY this tissue was destroyed?'] For Activity 3C, pose the reconnect question. [Wait 15 seconds of silence before cold-calling — enforce the wait.] Cold-call: 'Ochieng, what would happen if xylem was blocked?' [Wait 10 seconds.] 'Adhiambo, do you agree? What would you add?' [Wait 10 seconds.] Summarise on the board: 'Blocked xylem = water cannot reach leaves = turgor pressure drops = wilting. This is EXACTLY what the Kibwezi farmer is seeing — but WHY does this happen on a hot afternoon? That is our next question.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Jigsaw with Phenomenon Reconnect. The jigsaw structure ensures every student must deeply understand one system well enough to teach it — teaching is the highest level of processing. The phenomenon reconnect at the close prevents the lesson from becoming abstract anatomy; it forces students to apply the structural knowledge to the observable puzzle of wilting, maintaining the storyline thread.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Venn diagram contains accurate entries for at least 5 of 6 criteria — particularly correct on 'direction of transport' and 'living/non-living cells'. (2) During jigsaw, teacher listens for correct use of terms 'xylem', 'phloem', 'upward', 'bidirectional', and 'turgor' (introduced informally). (3) During reconnect discussion, at least 70% of students can verbally link xylem blockage to wilting with a correct causal chain. Teacher records misconceptions heard during jigsaw (e.g., 'phloem only goes down') on a sticky note for targeted correction at the start of Lesson 4.

framework.learnerExperience: Each student writes TWO sticky notes: (1) a BLUE sticky note stating one thing they now know about the plant transport system that they did NOT know at the start of the lesson (evidence-based claim, e.g., 'Xylem carries water upward through dead cells — no energy needed by the plant for this'); (2) an ORANGE sticky note stating one NEW question that today's lesson has raised for them (e.g., 'If xylem is made of dead cells, what force pulls the water all the way up to the top leaf of a tall maize plant?'). Students post their sticky notes on the class DQB under two columns: 'Evidence we now have' (blue) and 'New questions we need to answer' (orange). Teacher reads 3–4 orange notes aloud and says: 'These are exactly the questions that real plant biologists ask. Lessons 4 and 5 will give us the evidence to answer them.' Teacher draws a visible arrow on the DQB connecting today's blue notes to the original driving question, showing progress in the evidence-gathering sequence.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes (or direct students to tear notebook paper into two pieces and colour the edges). Say: 'Blue note first — write one EVIDENCE-BASED claim. It must include the word xylem OR phloem. You have 90 seconds.' [Wait 90 seconds.] 'Orange note — write the most burning question today's lesson has left you with. If you have no questions, you are not paying close enough attention.' [Wait 90 seconds.] 'Come up row by row and place your notes.' After posting: Read 3 orange notes aloud expressively. Say: 'Whoever wrote this — thank you. This question is going to drive our next two lessons. Leave it on the board where everyone can see it.' Point to the DQB driving question banner: 'How does water and food travel through an entire plant and why does this journey sometimes fail?' Say: 'We now know WHAT the highways are. Our next question is: what is the ENGINE that drives water up the xylem highway against gravity?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Claim-Evidence-Question DQB Protocol. Requiring students to write an evidence-based claim (not just 'I learned about xylem') builds the habit of grounding knowledge in evidence — a core science practice. The new-question sticky note externalises metacognition and creates a living, visible record of the class's growing understanding across the 8-lesson sequence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: Blue sticky notes contain a specific, accurate claim that references either xylem or phloem and at least one of: direction, substance transported, or cell type. Orange sticky notes show genuine intellectual curiosity about mechanism (force, energy, why) rather than definitional questions ('what does phloem mean?'), indicating students are ready for the mechanistic lessons ahead. Teacher photographs the DQB at end of lesson for the ARES platform record.

framework.learnerExperience: Students open a dedicated 'Plant Transport Model' page in their science notebooks (or use the printed model template provided on the ARES platform). They draw or paste a simplified outline of a whole maize plant. Using the colour conventions established today (blue = xylem pathway, red = phloem pathway), they: (1) draw the two vascular tissues as parallel lines running from root to leaf tip; (2) label what each tissue carries; (3) draw directional arrows; (4) add one annotation box per tissue explaining one structural feature (e.g., 'xylem = dead hollow cells, no nucleus, form continuous tubes'); (5) draw a thought-bubble next to the wilting leaf with the current best explanation for wilting: 'Insufficient water reaching leaf cells via xylem → loss of turgor pressure → wilting.' Students label this 'MODEL v.1 — Lesson 3' and leave a large blank margin/space titled 'What I will add next lesson' — reinforcing that this is a living, growing model.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'The last 8 minutes belong to your model. This is the most important thing you will do today — not because it is perfect, but because YOU are building it and you will add to it every lesson until it answers the farmer's full puzzle.' Project the model template on the board. Walk through the 5 steps one at a time: 'Step 1 — draw your maize plant outline. 30 seconds.' [Wait.] 'Step 2 — draw two parallel lines from root to tip. Label them. 30 seconds.' [Continue for each step.] With 2 minutes remaining: 'In your thought bubble, write your best explanation for the wilting — use the words xylem and turgor pressure.' [Circulate and give specific affirmations: 'Kamau, your arrow direction is exactly right. Fatuma, I love that you added the maize cob as a phloem sink — well done.'] Close: 'Label this Model v.1. In Lesson 4 we will add the mechanism that pulls water UP through the xylem — and your model will become even more powerful.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Progressive Model Revision (Cumulative Science Notebook Model). By explicitly versioning the model (v.1, v.2…) students experience science as an iterative, evidence-driven process rather than a fixed body of facts. The blank 'What I will add next lesson' margin creates anticipatory scaffolding — students enter Lesson 4 already wondering what belongs in that space, priming attention and motivation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: Model v.1 contains (a) correctly colour-coded xylem and phloem pathways; (b) directional arrows consistent with Phase 2 diagram work; (c) at least one accurate structural annotation per tissue; (d) a thought-bubble explanation that correctly links xylem, water, turgor pressure, and wilting in a causal chain — even if incomplete. Teacher uses a rapid 3-point holistic rubric (3 = all four elements present; 2 = three elements; 1 = fewer than three) during final circulation to identify students needing a check-in at the start of Lesson 4.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider the following reflection questions: (1) DIAGRAM ACCURACY: When I circulated during Phase 2, how many students had the phloem arrow direction wrong (downward only, missing the sink-directed upward flow to the maize cob)? What does this tell me about how I introduced bidirectional flow — did I give enough time for this to sink in, or did I rush the reveal? (2) JIGSAW QUALITY: During the jigsaw in Phase 3, did the 'xylem' pairs actually teach the passive/active energy distinction accurately, or did they skip it? If most pairs skipped row four of the Venn diagram, I need to re-address the energy requirement of xylem transport explicitly at the start of Lesson 4 before moving to transpiration pull. (3) DQB ORANGE NOTES: What proportion of students' new questions were mechanistic ('what force?' 'what happens inside the cell?') vs. definitional ('what does xylem mean?')? A high proportion of definitional questions signals that vocabulary load was too heavy and I should build in more explicit vocabulary scaffolding in Lesson 4. (4) MODEL v.1 QUALITY: Did students' thought bubbles show a genuine causal chain (xylem → water → turgor → wilting) or just a label ('wilting because no water')? Shallow thought bubbles indicate students can recall facts but cannot yet reason causally — a target for Lesson 4 questioning. (5) TIMING: Was 80 minutes sufficient for all five phases? If Phase 2 ran long (a common occurrence with diagram work), which phase was compressed, and how did that affect the quality of the DQB and Model phases? Plan to use a visible countdown timer in the next lesson.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed a whole-plant diagram of a maize plant (rooted in Kibwezi soil) showing two distinct vascular systems — xylem and phloem — running continuously from root tip to leaf tip and to the developing maize cob. They observed that blue (xylem) arrows point strictly upward, while red (phloem) arrows are bidirectional — primarily downward but also toward the cob as a sink. They observed from the reading that xylem is composed of dead, hollow cells requiring no living machinery to function, while phloem is made of living cells.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that plants have two specialised vascular transport tissues: (1) Xylem — transports water and dissolved mineral salts from roots upward to leaves; composed of dead, lignified cells that form continuous hollow tubes; transport is unidirectional (upward). (2) Phloem — transports manufactured food substances (sugars/assimilates) from leaves to all other parts of the plant including roots and storage organs (e.g., maize cob); composed of living sieve tube cells; transport is bidirectional (source to sink). Students learned to locate both tissues within root, stem, and leaf cross-sections and to represent their directional flow using a standardised colour-coding system.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students used their new knowledge of xylem and phloem to deepen their explanation of the Kibwezi wilting maize phenomenon: the xylem highway must continuously deliver water from the soil (absorbed by root hairs via osmosis, as established in Lesson 2) upward through the stem to the leaves. If this upward delivery is insufficient — whether due to high evaporative demand on a hot afternoon, a long path length in tall maize, or competition from the dry riverbed soil — the leaf cells lose turgor pressure and the plant wilts. This explains why the farmer's morning watering does not guarantee afternoon hydration of the topmost leaves. Students recorded this causal chain in Model v.1 and posted evidence claims on the Driving Question Board, advancing the class's collective answer to the driving question.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: xylem, phloem, vascular tissue, plant transport, water transport, mineral salts, food transport, translocation, bidirectional flow, unidirectional flow, turgor pressure, wilting, maize, Kibwezi, whole-plant diagram, cumulative model, colour-coded annotation, Venn diagram, jigsaw strategy, driving question board, Grade 10 Biology, CBE Kenya, KICD 2.2

LESSONS.title: The Entry Point: Root Structure and Water Absorption
LESSONS.aresKeywords: root hair cells, osmosis, active transport, cortex, endodermis, xylem, water potential, semi-arid, wilting, Kibwezi, mineral salts, surface area, root structure, turgidity

slo.purpose: To explain how water and mineral salts enter the root of a plant, linking root structure to the mechanisms of osmosis and active transport.
slo.knowledge: Students will know: (1) the structural features of root hair cells and how they increase surface area for absorption; (2) the pathway of water from soil through root hair, cortex, endodermis, and into xylem; (3) that water enters by osmosis (passive) and mineral salts enter by active transport (requiring energy); (4) the role of water potential gradients in driving osmosis.
slo.skills: Students will be able to: (1) prepare and observe a hand-lens drawing of a maize or bean root tip, labelling root hairs, cortex, endodermis, and xylem; (2) set up and record results from a potato-strip osmosis experiment; (3) construct a labelled diagram showing the pathway of water entry into the root; (4) use evidence from the osmosis experiment to support an explanation of wilting in Kibwezi.
slo.attitudes: Students will appreciate the precision of biological structures — that the microscopic root hair cell is the critical gateway determining whether a maize plant in Kibwezi survives or wilts, fostering wonder at the relationship between form and function.
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lessons 1–3 established what is happening in the Kibwezi maize plants (wilting despite watered soil) and introduced the vascular transport system at a macro scale. Lesson 4 zooms in to the entry point: the root. Students now gather direct observational and experimental evidence for HOW water actually crosses from soil into the plant — the osmosis mechanism — and why that mechanism can fail under dry, saline, or high-transpiration conditions. This evidence is the mechanistic foundation for understanding the full journey of water in Lessons 5–8.
slo.safetyNotes: • Handle scalpels/razor blades for cutting potato strips only under teacher supervision; always cut away from the body on a cutting board. • Wash hands after handling soil and roots. • If using iodine stain for root sections, avoid contact with skin and eyes — rinse immediately with water if contact occurs. • Salt solutions are non-toxic but label all beakers clearly to avoid confusion. • Dispose of potato strips and root material in the compost/bin — not in the sink.

LESSONS.overview: In this 80-minute double lesson, students first revisit the Kibwezi wilting puzzle with a targeted prediction: if water is in the soil, what is stopping it from entering the maize root? They then observe real maize or bean seedling roots (grown in the school garden) under hand lenses and, where possible, under a microscope, producing scientific drawings of root hair cells and the internal cross-section of the root. Next, they conduct a structured osmosis investigation using potato strips in tap water versus concentrated salt solution — a direct analogue for root-hair cells in soil versus salty soil — and record quantitative data (mass or length changes).
