In the Explain phase, students use their observations and data to construct a causal explanation: water moves by osmosis down a water-potential gradient from soil into root hair cells, then across the cortex and through the endodermis into the xylem, while mineral salts move against their concentration gradient via active transport requiring ATP. Students then update the class Driving Question Board (DQB) and add the 'Root Entry' module to their cumulative transport model, explicitly connecting their findings back to why the Kibwezi farmer's maize wilts even when she has watered.

framework.learnerExperience: Students receive a short written prompt (displayed on the board or ARES screen): 'The Kibwezi farmer watered her maize field at 7 a.m. By noon the plants are wilting. Water IS in the soil — you can see it. Write your best explanation: what do you think has to happen for that soil water to actually get INSIDE the root and travel upward? What might be stopping it?' Students write individual predictions in their science notebooks for 4 minutes, then share with a partner (Think-Pair-Share). Selected pairs share with the class. The teacher records key prediction words on the board in two columns: 'What helps water enter' and 'What might block water entry.' Students also revisit their cumulative model from Lesson 3 and annotate it with a question mark at the root zone, labelling their prediction.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the prompt and say: 'Before we touch any equipment today, I want you to think hard about the root — the entry point. Look at your model from last lesson. There is a gap here at the root. Today we fill it with real evidence.' Allow 4 minutes of silent individual writing. Circulate and note 3–4 interesting or contrasting predictions without commenting. After pair discussion (2 minutes), cold-call exactly 4 pairs: 'Pair 1 — Akinyi and Mwangi — what did you predict? Pair 2 — Fatuma and Otieno — do you agree or disagree, and why?' Record responses verbatim on the board. Explicitly say: 'I am NOT telling you if you are right. We are going to use evidence to test these ideas.' WAIT TIME: after each pair responds, wait 10 seconds before calling the next pair to allow class to process. Circle the word 'osmosis' if any student uses it; if none do, write it on the board with a question mark and say: 'Some biologists use this word to describe water movement — keep it in mind as we observe today.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share Prediction Elicitation — by externalising prior conceptions before observation, students create a cognitive 'gap' between what they think and what the evidence will show. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and makes conceptual change visible at the end of the lesson when students revisit the board.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans notebook predictions during circulation. Look for: (1) Does the student invoke any mechanism (pressure, sucking, diffusion, osmosis) or only describe the outcome? (2) Does the student connect root structure to function? Students who only write 'the water goes up through the roots' need targeted questioning during the Observe phase. Collect one notebook prediction snapshot mentally from a struggling learner to scaffold during Phase 2.

framework.learnerExperience: PART A — Root Observation (25 minutes): In groups of 4, students receive a tray containing: 2–3 maize or bean seedlings (7–10 days old, grown in the school garden or in soil-filled cups), a hand lens, a ruler, and a prepared worksheet with a blank circle for a scientific drawing. Students: (1) gently remove one seedling from soil and rinse the root under a small cup of water; (2) use the hand lens to observe root hairs — they sketch and label what they see, measuring the approximate length of root hairs in mm; (3) where a microscope is available, the teacher (or a trained student) has pre-prepared a thin cross-section of a root stained with dilute iodine, mounted on a slide — groups rotate to view it and add to their drawings, labelling root hair cell, cortex cells, endodermis, and central xylem. Where no microscope is available, students use a printed ARES diagram of a root cross-section and annotate it. PART B — Osmosis Investigation (20 minutes): Each group receives 4 potato strips of equal length (approx. 5 cm) cut from a raw Irish potato. Two strips go into a beaker of tap water; two go into a beaker of concentrated salt solution (2 tablespoons of table salt per 250 ml water — a proxy for dry, salty soil). Students measure and record initial length/mass of each strip, leave for 15 minutes while completing Part A drawings, then measure final length/mass and record observations (turgid vs. flaccid, firm vs. floppy). Students record all data in a results table in their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves: Before Part A begins, hold up a seedling and say: 'This maize seedling was growing in our school garden — the same species as the Kibwezi farmer's crop. You are going to look at the part of the plant that is SUPPOSED to bring in water. Look carefully. What do you notice?' WAIT 10 seconds. Circulate during drawing; prompt with: 'How many root hairs can you count on 1 cm of root? Why might having MORE root hairs matter for the Kibwezi farmer's maize?' For microscope rotation, say: 'You have 90 seconds at the microscope. Focus on finding the xylem in the centre. Point to it on your drawing.' For Part B setup, demonstrate cutting one potato strip and say: 'All strips must be the same length — this is our controlled variable. Why?' Cold-call 2 students to answer. After 15 minutes, prompt groups: 'Before you measure, PREDICT: which strips will be longer — water or salt solution? Write your prediction NOW.' Then have them measure and record. Ask: 'Which strip feels like the maize stem on a good morning? Which feels like the wilted maize at noon in Kibwezi?' WAIT 12 seconds. Cold-call 3 groups to report their results aloud; record class data in a shared table on the board.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Observation with Annotated Scientific Drawing — requiring students to produce a labelled drawing (not just look) forces active processing of structural features. Connecting the potato strip experiment directly to the Kibwezi phenomenon ('which strip feels like the wilted maize?') prevents the investigation from becoming an isolated activity; it is always evidence for the driving question. The shared class data table on the board makes patterns visible across groups, prompting discussion of variability and reliability.
framework.formativeAssessment: Circulate and check drawings: every drawing must include at least 3 labels. If a student labels only 'root hair' and nothing else, prompt: 'What is INSIDE the root behind the hair? Can you find it on the cross-section?' For the osmosis experiment, listen for causal language: a student who says 'the salt one got shorter because salt took the water' is beginning to apply osmosis correctly. A student who says 'the salt one dried out' has an incomplete model — note this for the Explain phase. Collect class data table results — if more than 2 groups show unexpected results (e.g., salt strip got longer), pause and troubleshoot together before moving on.

framework.learnerExperience: Students first complete a Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) frame in their notebooks: CLAIM: 'Water enters the root by...' EVIDENCE: (cite their potato strip data and root hair observation) REASONING: (explain WHY the evidence supports the claim, using the concept of water potential / concentration gradient). The teacher then leads a whole-class explanation using a large diagram of a root cross-section drawn on the board, annotating it live as students contribute. Key concepts built: (1) water potential gradient — soil water (high) → root hair cell (lower) → cortex (lower) → xylem (lowest) drives osmosis passively; (2) the Casparian strip in the endodermis forces all water through the living cells (symplastic pathway) — a checkpoint; (3) mineral salts move against their concentration gradient into root hair cells via active transport — this requires ATP from root cell respiration; (4) connection back to Kibwezi: in dry, salty, or compacted soil, the water potential of the soil may be LOWER than the root hair cell, reversing or stopping osmosis — this is why the farmer's maize wilts even with some soil water present if the soil salinity is high or the root hairs are damaged. Students then return to their predictions from Phase 1 and annotate them: 'My prediction was ✓ correct / ✗ incorrect / ~ partially correct because...'
framework.teacherMoves: Start the Explain phase by saying: 'You now have two pieces of evidence — your root drawing and your potato strip data. A scientist does not just describe what happened; they explain WHY. Let's build that explanation together.' Write on the board: WATER POTENTIAL — say 'This is the key idea. Water always moves from where it has MORE potential energy to where it has LESS — like water flowing downhill. In Kibwezi, we need to ask: is the water in the soil at higher or lower potential than inside the root hair cell?' Cold-call 3 students for responses. WAIT 10 seconds each time. As students contribute, draw arrows on the root diagram showing the direction of water movement. When you reach the endodermis, say: 'Here is something surprising — there is a waxy barrier here called the Casparian strip. It FORCES water to go through a living cell. Why might the plant WANT to control what gets in here?' WAIT 12 seconds. Cold-call 2 students. For mineral salts, ask: 'If mineral salts are in low concentration in soil and the plant needs them in high concentration inside — can they move by osmosis? No — they move by active transport. This costs the plant energy. Where does that energy come from?' Accept 'respiration' and elaborate. Conclude by returning to Kibwezi: 'If the farmer's soil becomes very salty — perhaps from poor-quality irrigation water — what happens to the water potential of the soil? Does water enter the root or leave it?' WAIT 15 seconds. Cold-call 2 students. Summarise on the board: OSMOSIS (passive, water potential gradient) + ACTIVE TRANSPORT (energy, mineral salts) = ROOT ABSORPTION.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) Framework — CER requires students to explicitly link their empirical data (potato strip results, root hair observations) to conceptual claims (osmosis, active transport), and to articulate the logical reasoning that connects them. This combats the common error of treating the experiment as separate from the biology concept. Live annotated diagram building ensures the explanation is co-constructed, not just transmitted, increasing retention and ownership.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect CER notebooks from 6 students (2 high, 2 middle, 2 lower based on Phase 1 assessment) and scan during Phase 4 DQB work. Indicators of mastery: (1) Claim uses the word 'osmosis' correctly; (2) Evidence cites specific data ('the strip in salt solution decreased in length by 1.2 cm'); (3) Reasoning mentions 'water potential' or 'concentration gradient' and direction of movement. Indicator of misconception: 'Water was absorbed by osmosis AND active transport' — the student has confused the mechanisms for water vs. mineral salts. Address this in Phase 4 discussion.

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the class Driving Question Board. Working first individually (2 minutes) then as a class (5 minutes), students: (1) identify which questions on the DQB they can now ANSWER with today's evidence — they write answers on green sticky notes and add them to the board; (2) identify NEW questions today's lesson has raised — written on yellow sticky notes; (3) revise any predictions or partial answers from Lessons 1–3 that today's evidence updates. Specifically, students address: 'Why does the Kibwezi farmer's maize wilt even when soil has water?' — students should now be able to give a partial mechanistic answer: 'If soil water potential is lower than root hair cell water potential (e.g. due to salt or dry compacted soil), water cannot enter by osmosis, so the plant wilts despite water being present.' Students also address: 'Why do plants near the dry riverbed wilt sooner?' — root hairs in drier, more saline sandy soil near the riverbed may face unfavourable water potential gradients earlier.
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the DQB and say: 'In Lesson 1 we put this question up: Why does the Kibwezi maize wilt even though water is in the soil? We now have real evidence. Turn to your partner — can you answer ANY part of this question using what you found today? You have 2 minutes.' After pair discussion, cold-call 3 students to place their green sticky notes on the board and read their answers aloud. For each answer, ask the class: 'Does everyone agree? Is there anything missing from this explanation?' WAIT 10 seconds. Then ask: 'What NEW questions has today's lesson given you? What do we STILL not know?' Prompt if needed: 'We know water enters the root — but how does it travel UP to a leaf 2 metres high in a maize plant? Does it just… sit there? Add that question if it's not already on the board.' Ensure the board visually tracks the progression: Lessons 1–3 evidence cluster vs. today's new evidence cluster.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Iterative Update — the DQB makes the epistemological progress of the class visible and cumulative. By distinguishing 'answered' (green) from 'new questions raised' (yellow), students experience science as a process of progressively deeper understanding rather than a checklist of facts. Anchoring every DQB update to the Kibwezi phenomenon ensures the board remains a living narrative, not a vocabulary list.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read green sticky notes posted: check that students are citing mechanisms (osmosis, water potential, active transport) not just restating the phenomenon ('water goes in'). A sticky note that reads 'Water enters by osmosis because the root hair cell has lower water potential than the soil' scores at mastery. A note that reads 'Water enters through root hairs' is descriptive only — prompt that student: 'Can you add HOW it enters and WHY it moves that direction?' Count the number of new yellow-note questions — a rich set of questions (e.g. 'How does water go up against gravity?', 'What happens to root hairs in very dry soil?') indicates high cognitive engagement.

framework.learnerExperience: Students retrieve their cumulative transport model (started in Lesson 1 and updated each lesson). Today they add a detailed 'Root Entry Module' to the model. Using a template provided on ARES (or drawn freehand), they draw and annotate: (1) soil particles with water surrounding them (labelled 'high water potential'); (2) a root hair cell with its elongated extension, labelled with a lower water potential; (3) arrows showing water moving by OSMOSIS from soil → root hair → cortex cells → endodermis (with Casparian strip labelled) → xylem; (4) a separate arrow showing mineral ions entering by ACTIVE TRANSPORT with an ATP symbol; (5) a 'Kibwezi failure zone' annotation — a red dotted arrow showing what happens when soil water potential drops below root hair cell water potential (water moves OUT — the plant wilts). Students also write a one-sentence 'model statement' at the bottom of their model page: 'Water enters the root by osmosis along a water potential gradient, while mineral salts enter by active transport; in Kibwezi's dry season, falling soil water potential can reverse or stop osmosis, causing the maize to wilt.'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute or display the model template. Say: 'Your model is your running scientific explanation of the Kibwezi puzzle. Every lesson, it gets more complete. Today you add the entry point — the root. This must connect to what is already on your model from Lessons 1–3.' Circulate and check that: (1) arrows for osmosis and active transport are in the CORRECT direction; (2) the Casparian strip is included; (3) the 'Kibwezi failure zone' annotation is present. If a student omits the Casparian strip, say: 'What forces water to pass through a living cell and not around it? You observed that structure today — add it.' Cold-call 2 students to present their model to the class in the final 3 minutes, specifically asking them to 'point to where the Kibwezi farmer's problem starts.' Assign the model as a take-home completion task if not finished in class. Preview Lesson 5: 'Your model now shows water IN the xylem at the bottom of the root. Next lesson, we ask: how does it travel up — against gravity — to a leaf three metres high in a tall maize plant? Start thinking about that question tonight.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Annotated Model Revision — the running model functions as an external cognitive artefact that represents the student's growing mechanistic understanding. By requiring a 'Kibwezi failure zone' annotation, the teacher ensures the model is always explanatory (it must account for the phenomenon) not merely illustrative. The one-sentence 'model statement' forces students to synthesise visual and verbal representations, deepening encoding.
framework.formativeAssessment: Exit check — before students leave, teacher does a rapid 'model gallery walk' of 6–8 open notebooks on desks. Check for: (1) Osmosis arrows pointing in the correct direction (soil → xylem); (2) ATP symbol present on active transport arrow; (3) 'Kibwezi failure zone' present with a direction-reversal annotation. Any model missing all three of these is flagged for a 5-minute catch-up conversation at the start of Lesson 5. Optionally, pose a verbal exit question to the whole class: 'In one word — what is the mechanism that pulls water from the soil into the root hair?' (Answer: osmosis). Cold-call 4 students for a rapid-fire round.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following: (1) OSMOSIS MISCONCEPTION CHECK — Did students correctly distinguish osmosis (water, passive, water potential gradient) from active transport (mineral ions, energy-requiring)? If more than a third of CER frames conflated the two, plan a 10-minute misconception-busting opener for Lesson 5 using the analogy of 'water sliding downhill freely vs. a person climbing uphill carrying a heavy load of minerals — the climber needs energy.' (2) POTATO STRIP DATA QUALITY — Did groups obtain consistent, measurable results (salt strip shorter/flaccid; water strip longer/turgid)? If results were inconsistent, evaluate whether salt concentration was too low or soaking time was insufficient. Adjust the protocol for future classes: increase salt to 3 tablespoons per 250 ml and extend soaking to 20 minutes. (3) DQB PROGRESSION — Count the green vs. yellow sticky notes. If yellow (new questions) outnumber green (answered questions) by more than 2:1, students may feel overwhelmed rather than progressive — validate this by framing new questions as 'scientific curiosity' at the start of Lesson 5. (4) KIBWEZI CONNECTION — Were students able to spontaneously apply today's mechanisms to the wilting phenomenon without being prompted? If not, consider adding a 'Kibwezi connector' card to Phase 3 with a sentence starter: 'This explains the Kibwezi farmer's problem because...' (5) EQUITY CHECK — Were students without microscope access equally engaged? If the printed ARES cross-section diagram was insufficient, explore whether a short 90-second video clip of a root hair cell under microscopy (downloadable for offline use via ARES) would bridge the gap for the next cohort.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed real maize/bean seedling roots under hand lenses and (where available) a microscope, producing labelled scientific drawings of root hair cells, cortex, endodermis, and xylem. They also observed potato strips in tap water vs. concentrated salt solution, recording changes in length/firmness after 15 minutes.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that water enters the root by osmosis — moving passively from soil (high water potential) through root hair cells, cortex, and endodermis into the xylem (low water potential). Mineral salts enter by active transport against their concentration gradient, requiring ATP. The Casparian strip in the endodermis forces all water through living cells, acting as a selective checkpoint. Root hairs increase surface area to maximise absorption rate.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students explained that the Kibwezi farmer's maize wilts despite soil water being present because, in dry or saline soil, the water potential of the soil drops below that of the root hair cells — reversing or halting osmosis so water cannot enter (or even leaves the root). Plants near the dry riverbed wilt sooner because sandy, drier, more saline soils near the riverbed reach this critical low water potential threshold faster than the wetter lower slopes.

LESSONS.title: The Highway: Stem Structure and Vascular Bundles

slo.purpose: To investigate the internal structure of herbaceous stems, identify xylem and phloem within vascular bundles, and connect stem anatomy to the movement of water and food through the plant — advancing students' understanding of why the Kibwezi maize wilts even after watering.
slo.knowledge: Students identify xylem and phloem within vascular bundles of herbaceous stems, describe their arrangement and structural differences, and distinguish between herbaceous and woody stem organisation.
slo.skills: Students prepare hand cross-sections of fresh stems using a razor blade, apply eosin or iodine stain, observe under a hand lens or microscope, draw and label vascular bundle diagrams, and set up the coloured water/celery experiment for Lesson 6.
slo.attitudes: Students appreciate the precision required in biological investigation, demonstrate care and patience when handling sharp instruments, and show curiosity about how microscopic structures solve the large-scale problem of moving water to the highest leaf of a maize plant.
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants? How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–4, students discovered that water enters roots by osmosis, moves through root hair cells, and is pulled upward by transpiration pull. A critical gap remained: what physical structure inside the stem actually forms the 'highway' that carries water upward and sugars downward? Lesson 5 fills that gap by revealing the vascular bundle — the anatomical core of the transport system. Students now see the tubes, not just the forces, and can begin to explain why a damaged or blocked stem (drought stress, disease, or waterlogging causing anaerobic root death) would break the highway and cause the Kibwezi maize to wilt irreversibly.
slo.safetyNotes: Razor blades are extremely sharp. Demonstrate the correct cutting technique before students begin: hold the stem firmly on a cutting tile, curl fingers away from the blade, and use a single smooth downward stroke. Students must not rush. Collect all blades at the end of the activity and store them in a labelled container. Eosin dye can stain skin and clothing — students should wear aprons if available and wash hands thoroughly after the activity. Iodine is a mild irritant; avoid contact with eyes.

LESSONS.overview: Lesson 5 is the anatomical investigation lesson in the eight-lesson evidence-gathering sequence. Students move from understanding the forces that drive transport (osmosis, transpiration pull — Lessons 1–4) to examining the physical structures through which transport occurs. Working in groups of three, students use razor blades to cut thin cross-sections of fresh bean or sunflower stems collected from the school garden, stain the sections with eosin or iodine solution, and observe vascular bundle arrangement with hand lenses or compound microscopes. They draw and label xylem and phloem, compare their findings to a herbaceous vs. woody stem diagram, and update their cumulative class transport model. At the end of the lesson, each group sets up the coloured celery or white carnation experiment (results observed in Lesson 6), making a written prediction about which tissues will be stained. The lesson is anchored throughout in the Kibwezi phenomenon: if the vascular bundles are the highway, what would block or destroy that highway and cause irreversible wilting?
LESSONS.aresKeywords: vascular bundle, xylem, phloem, herbaceous stem, cross-section, staining, transport pathway, eosin, cambium, epidermis, cortex, pith, celery experiment

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown two items side-by-side: a whole fresh bean stem (from the school garden) and a photograph of a wilting Kibwezi maize plant. The teacher poses the Predict prompt: 'We know water travels up the stem from root to leaf. But if you sliced this stem across — like cutting a sugarcane stalk — what do you think you would see inside? Draw a quick cross-section diagram in your notebook and label anything you predict you would find. Then write one sentence: how do you think the inside of this stem connects to the wilting maize in Kibwezi?' Students work individually for 5 minutes, then do a 'Turn and Compare' with a partner, noting similarities and differences in their predictions. The teacher cold-calls 4 pairs (8 students) to share predictions and records key ideas on the Driving Question Board under the column 'What we PREDICT about the stem highway.' Common predictions (hollow tube, solid green, a single big pipe, many small pipes) are recorded without correction — all are treated as hypotheses to be tested.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Hold up the fresh bean stem and the Kibwezi photograph together. Say: 'In our last lessons we discovered the FORCES that pull water up — osmosis, transpiration pull. Today we ask: what is the STRUCTURE, the actual physical highway, inside this stem?' 2. Display the Predict prompt on the board. Say: 'Take 5 minutes — draw, don't worry about being right. This is a hypothesis.' Set a visible 5-minute timer. 3. After 5 minutes say: 'Turn to your partner. Compare your diagrams. What did you agree on? What is different?' Allow 2 minutes. 4. Cold-call 4 pairs. For each, ask: 'Tell us your prediction and your reasoning.' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after asking before accepting answers. 5. Record all predictions on the DQB. Say: 'None of these are wrong yet — they are all hypotheses. Our job today is to gather evidence to test them.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-Observe-Explain (POE) — Activate prior knowledge and surface misconceptions before observation. Recording predictions publicly on the DQB creates accountability: students will return to their predictions after the dissection and must explain what changed and why. This strategy builds the habit of treating prior ideas as testable claims rather than fixed truths.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every student has a drawn cross-section diagram with at least one labelled prediction in their notebook before the dissection begins. The teacher scans notebooks during the Turn-and-Compare phase. Students who have drawn only a blank circle or written 'I don't know' are prompted with: 'Think about how water travels in a pipe at home — is there one big pipe or many small ones? Draw what you think.'

framework.learnerExperience:
Groups of three receive: one fresh bean or sunflower stem (10–12 cm), a razor blade, a cutting tile, a petri dish or bottle cap with eosin or iodine solution, a hand lens (and compound microscope if available), a slide and coverslip, and a printed diagram showing herbaceous vs. woody stem structure for reference (not revealed until after initial observation). Step 1 (8 min): Each group cuts 2–3 thin cross-sections of their stem. The thinnest section is placed in the petri dish with staining solution for 3–4 minutes. Step 2 (5 min): The stained section is placed on a slide with a drop of water, covered, and observed under the hand lens or low-power microscope objective. Students observe and describe in writing: colour, shape, arrangement of any distinct regions or bundles they can see. Step 3 (10 min): Students draw a large, detailed cross-section diagram in their notebooks (minimum 8 cm diameter), labelling everything they can actually see — they must distinguish between 'I can see this' and 'I am inferring this.' Step 4 (5 min): The teacher reveals the printed herbaceous stem diagram. Students add correct labels (epidermis, cortex, vascular bundle, xylem, phloem, cambium, pith) to their own drawing using a different coloured pen, noting where their predictions were correct and where they were surprised. The coloured celery stalks (each group receives one celery stalk split at the base into two, each half placed in a different colour of food dye) are set up in bottles on the windowsill. Students write a prediction: 'In Lesson 6, I predict we will see colour in _____ because _____.'

[KENYA CONTEXT: The teacher notes that the vascular bundles in a maize stem are scattered — different from the ring arrangement in bean stems — and draws a quick comparison: 'Think of a maize ugali — it comes from a monocot. Bean stew comes from a dicot. Even their stems are built differently!']
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Distribute materials. Demonstrate the cutting technique FIRST with your own stem at the front: 'Watch carefully — I hold the stem flat, fingers curled, one smooth stroke downward. Now you try.' Allow one practice cut per group on a spare stem piece before cutting the real sample. 2. Circulate during staining and observation. Ask at each table: 'What colours do you see? Where are the coloured patches concentrated? What shape are they?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds after each question before offering a hint. 3. Prompt the distinction: 'Write two columns — WHAT I SEE (observation) and WHAT I THINK IT IS (inference). This is what real biologists like Dr. Miriam Khamala at the Kenya Plant Health Inspectorate Service do when they examine plant tissue.' 4. When revealing the printed diagram, say: 'Using a different colour pen, add the correct labels. Circle anything that surprises you compared to your prediction.' 5. For the celery setup, say: 'This is your evidence-gathering experiment for next lesson. Write your prediction NOW, in a full sentence with a reason. Do not change it once you've written it.'  6. Cold-call 3 students to read their celery predictions aloud. WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after asking. Record predictions on the DQB.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Observation-Inference Separation — A structured two-column recording tool ('What I See' vs. 'What I Think It Is') trains students in the NGSS Practice of Distinguishing between Data and Interpretation. This prevents students from drawing diagrams of what they expect rather than what they observe — a common error in biological drawing tasks. The subsequent comparison to the printed reference diagram is used as a self-correction tool, not as the primary source of knowledge.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Each student's notebook contains a hand-drawn cross-section diagram with a minimum of four labelled structures, using two different pen colours to distinguish 'observed' from 'inferred/confirmed.' (2) Every group has a celery stalk set up in food dye with a written prediction recorded before the end of Phase 2. The teacher collects one notebook per group to check diagram quality and returns it before Phase 3. Red flag: diagrams copied directly from the printed reference without a personal observation drawing — teacher intervenes and asks the student to identify one specific thing they personally saw through the hand lens.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher leads a structured whole-class Jigsaw-to-Gallery discussion. Each group is assigned one of four 'expert' roles for 8 minutes: Group A — Xylem experts (structure, wall thickness, dead cells, role in water transport); Group B — Phloem experts (structure, sieve tubes, companion cells, role in sugar transport); Group C — Vascular Bundle Arrangement experts (ring in dicots vs. scattered in monocots, comparison of bean and maize); Group D — Connection-to-Phenomenon experts (how does the vascular bundle highway explain the Kibwezi wilting? What could block or destroy the highway?). Each expert group produces a 'mini-poster' (A4 paper, shared quickly at the front). Groups then regroup into mixed teams of four (one expert from each group) and teach each other for 8 minutes. Finally, the whole class comes together for a 4-minute Connect-Back: the teacher asks, 'Group D — what did you conclude? What could break the highway?' Cold-calls 3 students from different groups. Students individually write a two-sentence Explain statement: 'Water travels through _____ because _____. In Kibwezi, the maize might wilt permanently if _____ because _____.'
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Assign expert roles clearly. Say: 'You have 8 minutes as experts. Your job is to understand your structure or concept well enough to TEACH it. Use your diagram, the printed reference, and your textbook.' Set a timer. 2. Circulate during expert group work. For Group D (Phenomenon group), prompt: 'What would happen if the xylem tubes got blocked by air bubbles — what do we call that? What about if the roots rotted from waterlogging? Would the wilting be reversible or not?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds. 3. Signal regrouping: 'Move to your mixed groups. Each expert has exactly 2 minutes to teach — I will time you.' 4. Whole-class connect-back: 'Group D experts — in one sentence, how does today's anatomy lesson help explain the Kibwezi phenomenon?' Cold-call 3 students. WAIT TIME: 12 seconds each. 5. After each student speaks, ask the class: 'Does anyone want to add or challenge that?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. 6. Say: 'Now write your own two-sentence Explain statement. This is individual — no copying from partners.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Jigsaw Cooperative Learning with Phenomenon Re-anchoring — The jigsaw structure ensures every student develops depth in one area and breadth across all areas, reflecting the NGSS Practice of Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information. The mandatory Group D (Phenomenon group) ensures that anatomical knowledge is not learned in isolation but is immediately connected back to the explanatory purpose: understanding the Kibwezi maize failure. The two-sentence Explain statement is a low-stakes writing-to-learn tool that makes thinking visible.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) The two-sentence Explain statement correctly names xylem as the water pathway and phloem as the sugar pathway, and gives a plausible mechanistic reason for irreversible wilting (e.g., permanent xylem blockage by air embolism, cavitation, or root destruction). (2) During the whole-class Connect-Back, the teacher listens for whether students use causal language ('because,' 'which means that,' 'this causes') rather than purely descriptive language ('xylem is a tube'). Students using only descriptive language are prompted: 'That's what it is — but WHY does that structure matter for the wilting maize?'

framework.learnerExperience: The class returns to the whole-group Driving Question Board. The teacher reads aloud the two main KICD Key Inquiry Questions — 'How are water and mineral salts transported in plants?' and 'How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?' — and the overarching driving question about the Kibwezi maize. Each student writes two sticky notes (or two entries on the class chart if sticky notes are unavailable): (1) One piece of NEW EVIDENCE gathered today that helps answer the driving question — must reference today's stem dissection or observation. (2) One NEW QUESTION that today's lesson raised — something they are still wondering about. The teacher reads several aloud and sorts them into three DQB columns: 'Evidence we now have,' 'Questions still open,' and 'Connections to the Kibwezi Phenomenon.' The teacher highlights the bridge to Lesson 6: 'Tomorrow we will check our celery experiment. The coloured water will have travelled through the stem overnight — and you will see with your own eyes which tissue carries the water. That will either confirm or challenge what you have concluded today.'
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Point to the DQB. Say: 'Look at what we predicted in Phase 1. Which predictions were correct? Which were wrong? Let's update honestly.' Cross out or annotate predictions on the DQB in a visible colour. 2. Distribute sticky notes (or ask students to write in their lesson journals). Say: 'Write TWO things — one piece of evidence, one new question. You have 3 minutes.' Set timer. 3. Cold-call 4 students to share their evidence statements. WAIT TIME: 10 seconds after each. Ask: 'Is this evidence or inference? How do you know?' 4. Cold-call 3 students to share their new questions. Post them in 'Questions still open.' Say: 'Good scientists always leave lessons with more questions than they started with — that is a sign of real understanding.' 5. Point to the celery stalks on the windowsill: 'These are working for us overnight. Write in your notebook: I predict that by tomorrow, the coloured water will be visible in _____ because _____.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Evidence-Question-Phenomenon Sorting — The three-column DQB structure (Evidence / Questions / Connections to Phenomenon) trains students in the NGSS Practice of Engaging in Argument from Evidence by requiring them to distinguish between what they now know (evidence), what they still need to find out (questions), and how it all connects to the real-world case they are trying to explain. Public posting of questions normalises intellectual uncertainty and creates shared curiosity for Lesson 6.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every student has contributed at least one sticky note or journal entry to each DQB column before leaving. The teacher scans entries for specificity — vague entries like 'I learned about stems' are redirected with: 'Can you name a specific structure you saw today and explain what it does for the plant?' The teacher photographs the updated DQB at the end of the lesson to track conceptual progression across the eight-lesson sequence.

framework.learnerExperience: Students retrieve their cumulative transport model — the annotated diagram they have been building since Lesson 1, showing the full journey of water from soil to leaf. In Lessons 1–4, the model already includes: root hair cells, osmosis arrows, the cortex pathway, and the xylem column in the root. Today, students add the stem layer: they draw and label a cross-section of the herbaceous stem on the side of their model, showing vascular bundles with xylem (inner, thick-walled, dead cells, coloured blue or pencil-shaded) and phloem (outer, thin-walled, living, coloured red or dotted). They draw arrows showing: water moving UP through xylem, sugars moving DOWN through phloem. They annotate: 'Xylem = one-way upward highway for water and minerals.' 'Phloem = two-way food highway, mainly downward from leaves.' Students also mark a 'DANGER ZONE' on their model — where they think the highway is most vulnerable to failure in a drought-stressed Kibwezi maize plant — and write a one-sentence justification. Groups of three briefly compare models (2 minutes) and agree on any corrections before the lesson ends.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Say: 'Take out your cumulative model. You are now going to add the stem to your plant transport highway. Use today's observation to make this accurate — not a copy from the textbook, but from what YOU saw.' Allow 5 minutes for individual model updating. 2. Circulate and check: are xylem and phloem correctly positioned (xylem inner/adaxial, phloem outer/abaxial in most herbaceous dicots)? Prompt incorrect models: 'Look at your cross-section diagram — which bundle tissue was closest to the centre? Which was closest to the outside?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. 3. Say: 'Now mark a DANGER ZONE — where do you think the highway is most fragile? Write one sentence explaining your choice.' Allow 2 minutes. 4. Say: 'Compare with your group for 2 minutes. If you disagree on the danger zone, keep both versions — we will test this idea in Lessons 6 and 7.' 5. Close by pointing to the wilting maize photograph: 'This farmer in Kibwezi is still waiting for an answer. Our model is getting closer to explaining her problem. What do we still need to know?' Accept 2 quick verbal responses. WAIT TIME: 10 seconds each.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision — The running annotated diagram is the cornerstone sensemaking artefact of the eight-lesson sequence, embodying the NGSS Practice of Developing and Using Models. Each lesson adds one layer of mechanistic detail, so students can see — literally, visually — how their understanding has grown. The 'DANGER ZONE' annotation forces students to apply their new anatomical knowledge predictively, bridging from structure to function to the real-world failure case. Comparing models in small groups surfaces persistent misconceptions before they are consolidated.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) The cumulative model now shows at minimum: root hair → cortex → xylem (root) → xylem (stem vascular bundle) → upward arrow to leaf, AND phloem with a downward sugar-transport arrow. (2) The 'DANGER ZONE' annotation is accompanied by a mechanistic justification (e.g., 'If xylem in the stem gets air-locked during extreme drought, water cannot reach the leaves and the plant wilts permanently — like in Kibwezi'). Models missing the phloem layer or showing only one undifferentiated 'tube' are flagged for follow-up at the start of Lesson 6.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions to inform Lesson 6 planning:
1. SAFETY EXECUTION: Did all students follow the razor-blade cutting protocol correctly? Were there any near-misses or rule violations? If so, adjust the Lesson 6 celery observation setup to minimise further blade use.
2. OBSERVATION QUALITY: When you collected notebooks during Phase 2, did students distinguish between their own observations and the reference diagram labels? If more than one-third of the class copied directly from the printed diagram without a personal observational drawing, begin Lesson 6 with a 5-minute 'blind observation' exercise — hand lens observation before any reference material is provided.
3. MISCONCEPTIONS SURFACED: The most common misconception in this lesson is that xylem and phloem are 'side by side as two separate tubes running the full length of the stem' rather than organised into discrete vascular bundles. Did this arise in DQB entries or model drawings? If so, use the celery cross-section in Lesson 6 to explicitly re-address it.
4. PHENOMENON CONNECTION: Did Group D (Phenomenon experts) produce a mechanistically rich explanation of how vascular bundle damage causes irreversible wilting, or did they stay at the descriptive level ('the tubes get blocked')? If only descriptive, plan a Lesson 6 opening hook: 'A sugarcane farmer in Kisumu notices that after a long dry spell, even when rain returns, some plants never recover — but some do. Using what you know about xylem and phloem, predict which damage would be reversible and which would not — and why.'
5. CELERY PREDICTIONS: Review the written celery predictions collected at the end of the lesson. How many students correctly predicted that only xylem would be stained? How many predicted the whole stem or the phloem? Use this data to plan targeted questioning during Lesson 6 observations — cold-call students whose predictions were wrong first, so they can experience productive cognitive conflict when they see the actual result.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students cut cross-sections of fresh bean or sunflower stems, stained them with eosin or iodine, and observed distinct coloured patches arranged in a ring pattern (in dicot stems). They noted that some regions had thicker walls and appeared more densely stained than others. They also set up split celery stalks in two different food-dye colours and observed the initial uptake of colour beginning at the cut base.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The stem contains vascular bundles made up of two distinct tissues: xylem (inner, thick-walled, dead cells that form hollow tubes for carrying water and minerals upward) and phloem (outer, thin-walled, living cells that carry dissolved sugars mainly downward from leaves). In herbaceous dicot stems the bundles are arranged in a ring; in monocot stems (like maize) they are scattered. These bundles are the physical 'highway' that connects roots to leaves.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Water and mineral salts absorbed from the soil by root hair cells travel upward through the xylem vessels in vascular bundles running through the stem to every leaf. Sugars manufactured in leaves by photosynthesis travel downward through the phloem to roots, growing tips, and storage organs. In the Kibwezi maize, extreme drought stress can cause air embolisms (cavitation) in the narrow xylem vessels, permanently breaking the water highway — which is why some plants never recover even after watering returns. Plants like the sukuma wiki shaded by the mango tree lose less water through transpiration, reducing the tension in their xylem and making cavitation far less likely.

LESSONS.title: The Destination: Leaf Structure and Transpiration

slo.purpose: To investigate leaf anatomy and the process of transpiration, and to explain how water loss through stomata drives the wilting of maize in Kibwezi.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) describe the internal and external structure of a leaf in relation to transport and water loss; (b) define transpiration and identify the structures through which it occurs; (c) explain factors that affect the rate of transpiration.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) prepare and observe a stomatal impression from a sukuma wiki or Ficus leaf using clear nail varnish; (b) record and interpret results of the coloured dye experiment from Lesson 5; (c) construct a labelled diagram of a leaf cross-section showing xylem, stomata, cuticle, and mesophyll.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates how microscopic leaf structures have large-scale consequences for crop survival, and develops curiosity about how environmental factors — shade, wind, humidity — interact with plant physiology.
slo.keyInquiry: How does water leave a plant, and why does the rate of this loss vary depending on conditions in places like Kibwezi?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–5, students traced water from the Kibwezi soil into roots (osmosis), up the stem (xylem/transpiration pull), and confirmed xylem transport using coloured dye. Lesson 6 closes the journey — students now investigate the DESTINATION: the leaf. They discover that the same stomata that let CO₂ in for photosynthesis are the primary exit point for water, and that the rate of this loss (transpiration) is what determines whether the farmer's maize wilts or survives. This lesson makes the wilting phenomenon fully explicable for the first time.
slo.safetyNotes: Clear nail varnish contains acetone-based solvents — ensure the classroom or laboratory is well ventilated. Students must not apply nail varnish near open flames. Handle razor blades or scalpels (for leaf cross-sections) only under teacher supervision; pass blades with the handle facing the recipient. Wash hands after handling plant material. Students with known skin sensitivities should wear disposable gloves.

LESSONS.overview: Students begin by reviewing the 'sweating leaf bag' demonstration set up on a branch in the school compound before the lesson — water droplets visible inside the bag provide immediate, concrete evidence of transpiration. They then record and interpret the coloured dye results from Lesson 5, confirming that xylem carries water from root to leaf. The main practical involves preparing a stomatal impression from a sukuma wiki leaf using clear nail varnish, peeling it off, and observing guard cells and stomata under a hand lens or microscope. Students dissect a fresh leaf to observe veins (vascular bundles), the waxy cuticle, and upper vs lower epidermis. Using all gathered evidence, they define transpiration, identify the factors that affect its rate, and explicitly link those factors to the Kibwezi afternoon conditions (high temperature, low humidity, wind). A structured class discussion uses a Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) frame to answer: "Why does the maize wilt even though the soil was watered?" Students update their Driving Question Board and revise their cumulative transport models to include leaf anatomy and stomata.

framework.learnerExperience: Students walk outside (or view a photograph projected on screen) to observe the 'sweating leaf bag' — a clear polythene bag tied tightly around a leafy branch of the school's mango or Ficus tree before school started. They observe water droplets on the inside of the bag. Back in class, students write a 2-sentence individual prediction in their notebooks answering: 'Where did the water inside the bag come from, and how did it get there?' They then share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think–Pair–Share). Teacher selects 4 students using cold-call to share with the whole class — targeting students who gave contrasting predictions (e.g., one who says 'rain condensed' vs one who says 'the leaf sweated it out').
framework.teacherMoves: Before class: tie a clear polythene bag tightly around a leafy branch in the school compound. At the start of the lesson, say: 'Before we open our books, I want you to walk to the mango tree/Ficus outside — or look at this photo — and observe this bag carefully. Do NOT touch it yet. You have 90 seconds. Look, think, write one observation.' After students return, say: 'Now write TWO sentences: Where did that water come from, and how did it travel there?' Allow 2 minutes of silent individual writing. Then: 'Turn to your shoulder partner — share your prediction. You have 60 seconds each.' Cold-call 4 students — deliberately select 2 with 'condensation from air' type answers and 2 with 'plant released it' type answers. Record both prediction categories on the board under the heading: 'OUR PREDICTIONS — we will test these today.' Say: 'Keep your prediction written down — we will return to it at the end of the lesson and see who was right, and WHY.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think–Pair–Share with Prediction Anchoring. By writing predictions individually first, students commit to a prior model before peer influence. Recording contrasting predictions on the board creates productive cognitive dissonance — students are motivated to find out who is right. This directly connects to the Kibwezi phenomenon: if a bagged leaf produces water, the plant is actively losing water, not just sitting passively.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions during pair-share. Observable indicator of readiness: student predictions reference either 'the leaf' or 'the plant' as the source (even if mechanism is wrong). Red flag: students who write only 'I don't know' — teacher pairs these students with a confident peer and circulates to prompt: 'What do you think is INSIDE the leaf that could become water?'

framework.learnerExperience:
Students carry out THREE interlocking observations in this phase:

(A) DYE EXPERIMENT RESULTS (from Lesson 5): Students retrieve their cross-sections of celery/maize stems soaked in red/blue food dye. Working in pairs, they observe which tissue is stained, draw a labelled diagram, and write one sentence: 'The dye moved through _____ tissue, which tells us that _____ carries water up the stem.' Groups share findings briefly (2 minutes).

(B) STOMATAL IMPRESSION PRACTICAL: Each student takes a fresh sukuma wiki leaf (or Ficus). They apply a thin layer of clear nail varnish to a 2 cm² patch on the LOWER surface of the leaf. After 2–3 minutes (while doing observation C), they peel the dried film off using a piece of clear adhesive tape, place it on a glass slide, and observe under a ×10 hand lens or the class microscope. Students draw what they see: guard cells, stomatal pore, epidermal cells. They repeat on the UPPER surface and compare stomata density. They record: 'Upper surface — number of stomata visible: ___. Lower surface — number of stomata visible: ___.' Students also observe the leaf surface by touch and note the waxy, slightly shiny feel of the upper cuticle.

(C) LEAF DISSECTION / CROSS-SECTION: Using a razor blade (teacher-supervised), students make a thin cross-section of the same sukuma wiki leaf midrib. They place it on a slide with a drop of water and observe under the hand lens. Students identify and label: upper epidermis, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, vascular bundle (xylem + phloem), lower epidermis, stomata. A printed reference diagram is provided for comparison.
framework.teacherMoves:
Distribute materials in pre-labelled trays: sukuma wiki leaves, clear nail varnish, clear tape, glass slides, hand lenses, razor blades in holders, printed reference cross-section diagrams, coloured pencils. Say: 'We have THREE observations to complete in the next 25 minutes. I will time each one. Listen carefully to instructions before touching any equipment.' 

For Observation A: 'Take out your dye stem from Lesson 5. In your notebook, draw what you see — which tissue is coloured? Write one sentence explaining what this tells us about water transport.' Allow 4 minutes. Cold-call 2 students: 'Tell me exactly which tissue is stained and what that means.'

For Observation B: Model the nail varnish technique yourself first: 'Watch — I apply a thin layer here on the LOWER surface, like painting. Not thick — thin. We wait 2–3 minutes, then peel with tape. While we wait, we start Observation C.' After peeling: 'Place your film on the slide. What do you see? Count the pore-like openings on upper vs lower surface. Record your numbers.' Allow 10 minutes total. Circulate and ask: 'Why do you think there are more stomata on the lower surface? Discuss with your partner — 30 seconds.'

For Observation C: 'With your blade FLAT and supervised by me, make a thin slice across the midrib. Water on slide. Observe. Use the reference diagram to help you label your drawing.' Allow 8 minutes. Say: 'I am looking for at least FIVE labelled structures in your diagram.' WAIT 10 seconds after each instruction before accepting questions.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Triangulated Evidence Building: Students simultaneously hold three pieces of evidence — dye in xylem (Lesson 5 confirmation), stomata visible on leaf surface (new), and leaf cross-section showing vascular bundles connecting to mesophyll (new). This triangulation strategy — named explicitly for students ('We are triangulating: three pieces of evidence pointing to the same conclusion') — mirrors how scientists build confidence in a claim. Each observation alone is suggestive; together they build a coherent mechanistic story of water moving from xylem into mesophyll cells and out through stomata.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks diagrams during circulation. Observable indicators: (1) Student correctly identifies stained tissue as xylem in observation A. (2) Student records MORE stomata on lower surface than upper in observation B — if equal or reversed, teacher asks 'Which side did you apply the varnish to?' (3) Student's cross-section diagram includes at least 4 correctly labelled structures. Red flag: students who cannot distinguish xylem from phloem in cross-section — teacher points to the vascular bundle and asks: 'Which of these two is closer to the upper surface? That is the xylem.'

framework.learnerExperience:
Teacher introduces the formal definition of transpiration using the evidence students have just gathered. Students work in groups of 4 to complete a Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) scaffold in their notebooks:

CLAIM: 'The water inside the polythene bag came from _________.'
EVIDENCE: Students list at least THREE pieces of evidence from today's observations (dye results, stomatal impressions, leaf bag water droplets).
REASONING: Students write 3–4 sentences connecting their evidence to the claim using the terms: transpiration, stomata, xylem, guard cells, water vapour.

Groups then tackle the Kibwezi question directly: 'It is 2:00 pm in Kibwezi. Temperature: 38°C. Wind: strong. Humidity: very low. Using what you now know about transpiration, explain in your group why the maize is wilting at this exact moment — even though the farmer watered the soil this morning.' Each group writes a group explanation (min. 5 sentences). Groups share out — teacher writes key phrases from each group on the board, building a class consensus explanation.

Teacher then presents the FOUR factors affecting transpiration rate using a Kenya-relevant comparison table on the board:
• Temperature (hot Kibwezi afternoon vs cool Kericho morning)
• Humidity (dry Makueni vs humid Lake Victoria shore)
• Wind speed (open maize field vs sheltered sukuma wiki under mango tree)
• Light intensity (midday sun vs shaded plot)
Students copy and annotate the table, adding arrows showing 'increases transpiration rate' or 'decreases transpiration rate' for each factor.
framework.teacherMoves:
Open the Explain phase by returning to the board predictions: 'Let us go back to what you predicted. [Point to board.] Some of you said condensation from air. Some said the plant released it. Now you have evidence. Which is supported? Turn to your group — 30 seconds — then I will cold-call.' Cold-call 3 students. Then say: 'The scientific term for what you are describing is TRANSPIRATION. Write this definition exactly: Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from a plant, mainly through the stomata of leaves.' Students write and underline.

For the CER task: 'In your groups of four, complete the Claim–Evidence–Reasoning scaffold on page ___ of your workbook. You must cite at least THREE pieces of evidence from TODAY's observations. You have 8 minutes — go.' Circulate. At 5 minutes, say: 'You should be writing your reasoning now — use these words: transpiration, stomata, xylem, guard cells.' After 8 minutes, cold-call one group to share their full CER. Ask the class: 'Do you agree with their reasoning? What would you add?' Allow 2 student additions.

For the Kibwezi group explanation: 'Now apply this to our farmer in Kibwezi. It is 2:00 pm. Hot. Windy. Dry. Explain exactly what is happening inside her maize plant RIGHT NOW.' Allow 6 minutes. Cold-call 2 groups. Construct the class consensus explanation on the board together. Say: 'This is the most important paragraph you will write in this unit — it answers our driving question for the first time completely.'

For the factors table: present each factor with a Kenya contrast. Ask: 'Why does the sukuma wiki under the mango tree barely wilt? [WAIT 12 seconds.] What factor has the mango tree changed?' Cold-call 2 students before explaining.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Framework: CER is named explicitly and its purpose explained — 'Scientists do not just state claims; they justify them with evidence and explain the logic connecting the two. We are doing exactly what a plant physiologist at the University of Nairobi would do.' CER is particularly powerful here because students now have three independent pieces of evidence (dye, stomata, leaf bag) converging on one mechanism. The Kibwezi application task forces transfer — students must move from abstract definition to causal explanation of the anchoring phenomenon, which is the deepest level of sensemaking.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect CER notebooks from 6 randomly selected students (one per group) during the transition to the next phase. Observable indicators of proficiency: (1) Claim correctly names transpiration/stomata as source of bag water. (2) Evidence section lists at least 3 specific observations (not generic statements). (3) Reasoning uses at least 3 of the 5 key vocabulary words correctly in context. (4) Kibwezi group explanation correctly identifies at least 2 factors (high temperature + low humidity OR high temperature + wind) as increasing transpiration rate beyond the rate of water uptake. Red flag for reteaching: students who explain wilting as 'the plant is thirsty' without referencing the rate mismatch between water uptake and water loss.

framework.learnerExperience: The class DQB is projected or displayed at the front. Teacher reads aloud the driving question: 'How does water and food travel through an entire plant — from the soil to the highest leaf — and why does this journey sometimes fail, causing crops like maize in Kibwezi to wilt and die?' Students individually review the sticky notes/questions they added in Lessons 1–5. Each student selects ONE question from the DQB that they can now answer fully or partially using today's lesson. They write a 2–3 sentence answer on a new sticky note (different colour — e.g., green = 'I can now answer this') and place it over or beside the original question. Additionally, if today's lesson has raised a NEW question, students write it on a yellow sticky note and add it to the board. The class reviews 4–5 answered questions together, and the teacher draws a large arrow on the board timeline showing: Soil → Root → Stem → Leaf → ATMOSPHERE, marking 'Lesson 6 — LEAF' as the final node in the water journey.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Look at our Driving Question Board. You have been adding questions since Lesson 1. Today, for the first time, we can answer the FULL journey — soil to atmosphere. Take 3 minutes. Find ONE question on the board that you can now answer. Write your answer on a GREEN sticky note and place it on the board.' After 3 minutes: 'Does anyone have a NEW question that today raised? Write it on yellow and add it.' Cold-call 3 students to read their green answers aloud. After each, ask the class: 'Do you agree? Is this a complete answer? What could make it stronger?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Then point to the board timeline: 'Let us draw the complete journey together.' Build the arrow: Soil → Root hair (osmosis, L2) → Root cortex → Xylem vessel (L3–4) → Stem xylem (L5) → Leaf xylem → Mesophyll cells → Stomata → Atmosphere (L6). Say: 'This journey — and where it fails — is the answer to our driving question. Next lesson, we will add phloem and food transport to complete the picture.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Progressive Disclosure on the DQB: The DQB functions as a cumulative public record of the class's evolving understanding. By physically moving from 'question' sticky notes to 'answer' sticky notes, students experience epistemic progress — they can SEE what they have figured out. The board timeline arrow makes the causal chain spatial and memorable. This strategy, named 'Storyline Mapping,' helps students see that science knowledge builds lesson by lesson toward a coherent explanation — not as isolated facts.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all new green sticky notes after class. Observable indicator: answer notes correctly reference stomata as the exit point and/or transpiration rate as the mechanism linking soil water to wilting. Red flag: green notes that say only 'water goes through stomata' without any reference to rate, conditions, or the Kibwezi context — these students need follow-up prompting at the start of Lesson 7.

framework.learnerExperience:
Students turn to their cumulative transport model — a hand-drawn diagram they have been revising since Lesson 1, showing the full plant cross-section from root hair to leaf tip. Working individually, they add the following new structures and annotations to their model:
1. Leaf cross-section box (zoomed inset): label upper epidermis, waxy cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll with air spaces, lower epidermis, stomata with guard cells, xylem and phloem in vascular bundle.
2. Arrow labelled 'water vapour exits as transpiration' pointing from stomata outward.
3. Annotations for factors: next to the leaf, write 'Rate increases with: HIGH temperature, HIGH wind speed, LOW humidity, HIGH light intensity.'
4. A red annotation connecting back to Kibwezi: 'At 2:00 pm in Kibwezi: all four factors are at maximum → transpiration rate >> water uptake rate → wilting.'
Students who finish early add a 'comparison inset' showing the sukuma wiki under the mango tree — annotating how shade reduces light intensity and temperature, decreasing transpiration rate, explaining why it barely wilts.
Peer review: each student swaps models with a partner. The partner checks for the 5 required structures and writes one 'strength' and one 'suggestion' on a sticky note attached to the model.
framework.teacherMoves:
Say: 'Open your transport model from Lesson 1. You are going to make it more complete than it has ever been. You have 10 minutes to add the leaf cross-section inset, the transpiration arrow, the four factors, and the Kibwezi annotation. I am looking for FOUR labelled structures in your leaf inset and the Kibwezi annotation specifically.' Project a partially completed model on the board — show the empty leaf inset with dotted borders — say: 'Your leaf inset should look something like this frame. Fill it in using your own diagram from today's practical.'

Circulate during model building. Stop at 5 minutes and say: 'Pause. Check — does your model show WHERE water exits the plant? If not, add that arrow now.' At 8 minutes: 'One more minute — make sure your Kibwezi annotation is written.' After 10 minutes: 'Swap with your partner. Read their model. Write one STRENGTH and one SUGGESTION on a sticky note. You have 3 minutes.' After peer review, cold-call 2 students: 'What was the best thing about your partner's model?' and 'What did you suggest they improve?' End with: 'Your model now shows the complete water journey. In Lesson 7, we will add the phloem — the food highway — and complete the full transport system.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Peer Review: The cumulative model strategy — used across all 8 lessons — externalises student thinking and makes conceptual growth visible over time. Each revision is additive: students do not start fresh but build on prior work, mirroring how scientific models evolve. Peer review introduces the scientific practice of critique and revision (NGSS SEP 6: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions; SEP 7: Engaging in Argument from Evidence). Named for students: 'Scientists constantly revise their models as new evidence arrives — you are doing exactly that today.'
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect 5–6 models at random at the end of class. Observable proficiency indicators: (1) Leaf inset contains at least 4 correctly labelled structures including stomata and xylem. (2) Transpiration arrow exits through stomata — not through the cuticle or upper epidermis. (3) Kibwezi annotation correctly identifies the rate mismatch (transpiration exceeds uptake) as the cause of wilting. (4) At least 2 of the 4 transpiration factors are correctly listed. Criterion for mastery: all 4 indicators present. Criterion for reteaching flag: transpiration arrow exits from wrong location, OR Kibwezi annotation attributes wilting to 'no water in soil' (prior misconception not yet resolved).

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions in your professional journal:

1. PHENOMENON CONNECTION: Could every student articulate — in their own words — why the maize in Kibwezi wilts in the afternoon even after morning watering? Did the CER task reveal students who still hold the misconception that 'watering = no wilting'? What will you do in Lesson 7's opening to address this?

2. PRACTICAL QUALITY: Did the stomatal impression technique work with the leaf species available? If sukuma wiki leaves gave unclear impressions, which alternative leaves in your school compound gave better results? Note this for next year. Did the nail varnish dry sufficiently in the 2–3 minute window, or do you need to adjust timing?

3. DYE EVIDENCE: Were students able to clearly identify stained xylem tissue from Lesson 5? If results were ambiguous, consider whether the dye concentration was sufficient (2 teaspoons of food colouring per 200 ml water is recommended) or whether stems were left in dye long enough (minimum 4 hours). Adjust Lesson 5 setup instructions accordingly.

4. DIFFERENTIATION: Did higher-ability students complete the sukuma wiki comparison inset (shaded plot)? Were lower-ability students supported in completing at least the 4 required leaf inset labels? Consider preparing a partially pre-labelled scaffold diagram for the next cohort.

5. TIME MANAGEMENT: The Observe phase (3 simultaneous observations) is the most time-pressured segment. Did 25 minutes feel sufficient? If not, consider moving the dye result recording to a warm-up activity before the lesson formally begins.

6. DRIVING QUESTION PROGRESS: Review the DQB green sticky notes. Are students' answers becoming more mechanistic (citing stomata, transpiration rate, xylem) or are they still descriptive ('water comes out of the leaf')? This tells you how ready students are for Lesson 7's phloem and pressure-flow content.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed water droplets inside a polythene bag tied around a leafy branch (sweating leaf bag demonstration), confirming that leaves release water vapour. They recorded results from the Lesson 5 coloured dye experiment, observing stained xylem tissue in stem cross-sections. They prepared stomatal impressions from sukuma wiki leaves using clear nail varnish and observed guard cells and stomatal pores under a hand lens, finding more stomata on the lower epidermis. They made cross-sections of the leaf midrib and identified the vascular bundle, epidermis layers, mesophyll, and waxy cuticle.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that transpiration is the loss of water vapour from a plant, mainly through stomata in the lower epidermis of leaves. They learned that xylem vessels carry water from the roots all the way to the leaf mesophyll cells, where it evaporates and exits through stomata as water vapour. They identified four factors that increase the rate of transpiration: high temperature, high wind speed, low humidity, and high light intensity — all of which are present in Kibwezi on a dry afternoon. They explained why the farmer's maize wilts: transpiration rate in the hot, windy, dry afternoon exceeds the rate at which roots can absorb water from the soil, causing a water deficit inside the plant.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students can now explain the complete water journey from soil to atmosphere: water enters root hairs by osmosis, travels through root cortex cells, enters xylem vessels, is pulled up the stem by the transpiration pull (tension created as water evaporates from leaves), reaches leaf mesophyll cells through vein xylem, evaporates into air spaces, and exits through stomata as water vapour. The wilting of the Kibwezi maize is explained by a rate mismatch: on hot, dry, windy afternoons, stomata are open for photosynthesis, and the four transpiration factors combine to drive water loss far faster than roots can replace it, causing cells to lose turgor and the plant to wilt. The sukuma wiki under the mango tree barely wilts because shade reduces both light intensity and temperature — two of the four factors — dramatically slowing transpiration rate.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: transpiration, stomata, guard cells, leaf anatomy, xylem, cuticle, epidermis, mesophyll, transpiration rate, water loss, wilting, vascular bundle, stomatal impression, plant transport, sukuma wiki, Kibwezi, maize, water vapour, factors affecting transpiration, CER framework

LESSONS.title: The Engine: Transpiration Pull, Cohesion-Tension, and Root Pressure

slo.purpose: To investigate and explain the mechanisms that drive upward water movement in plants — transpiration pull (cohesion-tension) and root pressure — and to use quantitative data to partially resolve why the Kibwezi maize wilts even after watering.
slo.knowledge: Students will be able to: (a) describe the cohesion-tension mechanism as the primary driver of water movement up the xylem; (b) explain how transpiration pull is generated at the leaf surface; (c) describe root pressure as a supplementary driver of upward water movement; (d) define water potential and explain how gradients drive movement from soil → root → stem → leaf → atmosphere.
slo.skills: Students will be able to: (a) set up and use an improvised potometer to measure water uptake rates under varied conditions; (b) collect, record, and graph quantitative data from the potometer investigation; (c) analyse graphs to identify patterns and draw evidence-based conclusions; (d) construct a written scientific explanation linking transpiration rate, water uptake rate, and wilting.
slo.attitudes: Students will: (a) appreciate precision and repeatability in data collection; (b) show curiosity about natural phenomena affecting Kenyan farmers; (c) demonstrate responsibility in handling shared laboratory equipment; (d) value the contribution of quantitative evidence in supporting or refuting a scientific explanation.
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is Lesson 7 of 8 in the evidence-gathering sequence. Students now have prior knowledge of xylem structure (Lesson 4), osmosis and root hair absorption (Lesson 5), and stomata function (Lesson 6). This lesson provides the mechanistic engine that connects all previous evidence: transpiration pull, cohesion-tension, and root pressure explain HOW water travels the full journey from soil to leaf. Students generate their own quantitative data using an improvised potometer, then use it to directly answer the anchoring phenomenon — the rate of transpiration in Kibwezi's hot, dry, windy afternoon exceeds the rate of water absorption, causing wilting even in watered soil. This lesson sets up Lesson 8, in which students will build their final cumulative model of plant transport.
slo.safetyNotes: Caution students to handle cut stems carefully — fresh cuts should be made under water using a razor blade or sharp knife; teacher should supervise all cutting. Warn students not to touch the lamp (heat source) directly. Petroleum jelly should not be placed near eyes or mouth. Students should wash hands after handling plant material. Ensure rubber tubing connections are secure before blowing or creating suction.

LESSONS.overview: Students use an improvised potometer — assembled from a freshly cut leafy stem (e.g., a sprig of bougainvillea or a succulent herb available locally), rubber tubing, a calibrated capillary tube, and a ruler — to measure the rate of water uptake under four conditions: (1) control (still air, no lamp), (2) fan/blowing wind, (3) lamp directed at leaves, (4) petroleum jelly applied to leaf surfaces. Working in groups of four, they record the distance the air bubble moves in the capillary tube over two-minute intervals, calculate uptake rates (mm/min), and plot bar graphs. They then use the CLAIM–EVIDENCE–REASONING (CER) framework to construct an explanation of the cohesion-tension mechanism. Finally, they return to the Driving Question Board and post a sticky note answering: "Why does the maize wilt even after the farmer waters the soil?" This lesson partially resolves the anchoring phenomenon and prepares students for the final cumulative model in Lesson 8.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown a diagram of a 10-metre-tall maize plant with soil at the base and a leaf at the top. The teacher poses: 'The farmer in Kibwezi watered her soil this morning. By 2 pm, the maize is wilting. The water is still in the soil — you can see it. What force is pulling water from the soil all the way up to the top leaf, 10 metres in the air? And what might be making that force stronger at 2 pm than at 6 am?' Students write individual predictions in their science notebooks using the sentence starter: 'I think water moves upward because… and I think the force is stronger at 2 pm because…' After 3 minutes of individual writing, students share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think-Pair-Share). The teacher cold-calls 4 pairs (not volunteers) to share, recording key prediction words on the board in two columns: FORCES PUSHING (e.g., 'roots push it up', 'pressure') and FORCES PULLING (e.g., 'leaves pull it', 'sun pulls water', 'evaporation'). Teacher does NOT correct predictions — all ideas are honoured and left on the board to be revisited.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Display the tall maize diagram on the board or projector. Say verbatim: 'Before we do any investigation today, I want to know what YOU already think. No wrong answers — this is your honest prediction.' 2. Distribute science notebooks. Give students exactly 3 minutes of silent individual writing — use a visible timer. Do not allow discussion during this phase. 3. Say: 'Now share your prediction with your shoulder partner. You have 90 seconds each — Partner A speaks first, then Partner B.' 4. After Think-Pair-Share, cold-call exactly 4 pairs (use the class list, not raised hands). Ask each pair: 'Tell us ONE force you think is involved and why.' Record responses in the two columns (PUSHING / PULLING) on the board. 5. After all 4 pairs share, say: 'Notice that some of you think something PUSHES water up, and others think something PULLS it. Keep that question alive — our investigation today will give us data to test both ideas.' 6. Point to the DQB and say: 'We already have questions on our board about why maize wilts and how water gets to the top. Today we will gather quantitative — number-based — evidence to help us answer those questions.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: THINK-PAIR-SHARE with PREDICTION SORTING: Students activate prior knowledge from Lessons 4–6 (xylem structure, osmosis, stomata) and commit to a prediction before seeing evidence. Sorting predictions into PUSH vs. PULL creates a conceptual tension that motivates the investigation. Recording predictions publicly means students can compare their thinking to evidence at the end of the lesson, making conceptual change visible.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions while circulating during individual writing time. Observable indicators of prior learning: (a) students reference osmosis or root pressure as a 'push' force (from Lesson 5); (b) students reference stomata or evaporation as a 'pull' (from Lesson 6); (c) students who cannot connect to prior lessons write vague predictions — teacher notes these students for targeted support during the Observe phase. Teacher does NOT grade predictions; they serve as a diagnostic baseline.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of 4 receive a pre-assembled improvised potometer: a freshly cut leafy stem (bougainvillea sprig or locally available herb) inserted into rubber tubing connected to a calibrated capillary tube (marked in mm with a permanent marker and ruler), with the open end of the capillary tube submerged in a small beaker of water.
