An air bubble is introduced into the capillary tube by briefly lifting the tube out of the water. Students observe the air bubble move as the stem takes up water. Each group tests TWO of the four conditions (conditions are distributed across groups so the whole class generates a full dataset): Condition A — Control (still air, no additional heat); Condition B — Fan or student blowing gently on the leaves for 2 minutes (simulates Kibwezi afternoon wind); Condition C — Desk lamp directed at leaves from 15 cm (simulates intense afternoon sun); Condition D — Petroleum jelly applied to the upper surface of all leaves (blocks stomata). For each condition, students record the distance the bubble moves (mm) every 2 minutes for 6 minutes, calculate the average rate (mm/min), and record in a results table. Groups then share data with the class on the board. ALL students copy the full class dataset and draw a bar graph (x-axis: conditions A–D; y-axis: water uptake rate in mm/min). Students annotate their graphs with one sentence explaining the pattern they observe.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Before distributing equipment, demonstrate the potometer setup to the whole class (3 minutes). Say verbatim: 'Watch how the air bubble moves — that bubble is your measurement tool. The faster it moves toward the stem, the faster the stem is taking up water. This is your evidence.' 2. Distribute equipment to groups. Say: 'You have 20 minutes. Your first job is to get a stable bubble and record your control reading. Do NOT apply the lamp or fan until you have your control data.' 3. Circulate every 3–4 minutes. At each group, crouch to eye level and ask one specific question: (a) 'What is your bubble doing right now — is that what you predicted?' (b) 'How are you making sure your reading is fair? What are you keeping the same?' (c) 'If the bubble is moving faster, what does that MEAN for water uptake?' 4. At the 18-minute mark, say: 'Two minutes to finish your last reading and calculate your average rate. Rate = total distance divided by total time.' 5. Invite one representative from each condition group to write their average rate on the class board. Give WAIT TIME of 10 seconds after all four values are posted, then ask: 'Before I say anything — what pattern do you notice in these four numbers? Write one sentence in your notebook.' Cold-call 3 students (not volunteers). 6. Say: 'Now draw your bar graph using the class data. X-axis: the four conditions. Y-axis: water uptake rate in mm/min. Title: Water Uptake Rate Under Different Conditions — Kibwezi Investigation.' Allow 8 minutes for graph drawing. 7. Ask: 'Write one sentence on your graph: which condition produced the HIGHEST water uptake rate, and what does that tell us about what drives water loss in Kibwezi at 2 pm?' Give 10 seconds WAIT TIME, then cold-call 2 students.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: STRUCTURED DATA COLLECTION + CLASS DATA POOLING: Each group generates data for one or two conditions, but every student analyses the full class dataset. This mirrors authentic scientific practice (distributed investigation, shared data) and ensures all students have quantitative evidence to use in the Explain phase. The bar graph transforms raw numbers into a visual pattern, making the relationship between environmental conditions (wind, heat, blocked stomata) and water uptake rate immediately visible and discussable.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks (a) that results tables are correctly formatted with units (mm and mm/min); (b) that students calculate rate correctly (distance ÷ time, not just distance); (c) that bar graphs have labelled axes, a title, and correct scale. Students who annotate their graph with a sentence linking 'high uptake rate' to 'high transpiration' show readiness for the Explain phase. Students who write only descriptive annotations ('the bar is tallest for wind') need a bridging question: 'Why do you think the stem is taking up MORE water when the fan is blowing?'

framework.learnerExperience: Using their potometer data, students apply the CLAIM–EVIDENCE–REASONING (CER) framework to construct a written explanation. The teacher first provides a short guided explanation (10 minutes) of the cohesion-tension mechanism using a drawn diagram: (1) Water evaporates from the mesophyll cells through stomata (transpiration); (2) This lowers water potential in the leaf, pulling water from adjacent xylem cells; (3) Because water molecules COHERE (stick together via hydrogen bonds), the entire water column in the xylem is pulled upward like a chain; (4) This creates TENSION in the xylem — a negative pressure; (5) At the root end, water enters by osmosis from the soil, replenishing the column. Teacher also briefly explains ROOT PRESSURE: at night or in moist conditions, active uptake of mineral ions into root cells lowers the water potential of root cells, drawing water in by osmosis, which can push water upward (evidenced by guttation). Students then write their CER explanation independently: CLAIM — 'Water moves up the xylem because…'; EVIDENCE — at least two specific data points from their potometer investigation (with units); REASONING — 'This supports the cohesion-tension mechanism because…', explicitly linking the data to the mechanism. Students share CER explanations with a partner, give one piece of feedback using the stem: 'Your reasoning would be stronger if you explained WHY [condition] increased the rate…'
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Say: 'Put your graphs in front of you — we are going to BUILD an explanation together using your data and some new vocabulary.' Draw the cohesion-tension diagram live on the board (do not use a pre-drawn poster — drawing it step-by-step slows the explanation to student thinking speed). 2. At each step of the diagram, PAUSE and ask a specific question: (a) After drawing stomata: 'From Lesson 6, what opens the stomata at 2 pm on a hot Kibwezi afternoon? Give me the word.' WAIT 10 seconds. Cold-call 1 student. (b) After drawing the water column: 'If water is evaporating from the top of this chain, what happens to the chain? Think about pulling a string from one end.' WAIT 12 seconds. Cold-call 2 students. (c) After drawing root entry: 'What process from Lesson 5 gets water INTO the root hair cell from the soil? Say the word.' WAIT 10 seconds. Cold-call 1 student. 3. Introduce the words COHESION and TENSION with a physical analogy: 'Imagine the water molecules in the xylem are like the students in this class holding hands in a line. If the student at the front is pulled forward, the whole line moves — that is cohesion-tension. The force doing the pulling is transpiration at the leaf.' 4. Say: 'Now write your CER. You have 10 minutes. Your CLAIM must start: Water moves up the xylem because… Your EVIDENCE must include at least TWO numbers from your potometer data. Your REASONING must use the words: cohesion, tension, transpiration, and water potential.' 5. After CER writing, say: 'Read your partner's REASONING section only. Use this stem: Your reasoning would be stronger if you explained WHY [blank] increased the rate…' Give 4 minutes for peer feedback. 6. Ask the class: 'Who received feedback that helped them improve their reasoning? What did your partner suggest?' Cold-call 2 students. 7. Return to the maize diagram from Phase 1. Say: 'Now look at your prediction from the start of the lesson. Were you in the PUSH column, the PULL column, or both? How has your thinking changed?' Give 2 minutes of silent reflection writing.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: CLAIM–EVIDENCE–REASONING (CER) FRAMEWORK: CER is a disciplinary literacy strategy that structures scientific argumentation. By requiring students to cite specific numerical evidence from THEIR OWN potometer data, the framework ensures the explanation is grounded in the lesson's investigation rather than being copied from a textbook. The peer feedback step makes reasoning quality visible and improvable. The return to initial predictions at the end makes conceptual change explicit and personally meaningful.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects CER written explanations at the end of class (or photographs them). Success criteria: (a) CLAIM correctly identifies transpiration pull (cohesion-tension) as the primary mechanism; (b) EVIDENCE cites at least two specific data points with units (e.g., 'Under wind conditions, uptake rate was 4.2 mm/min vs. 1.1 mm/min in the control'); (c) REASONING uses all four target vocabulary words (cohesion, tension, transpiration, water potential) correctly and logically links evidence to the mechanism. Students who include root pressure in their explanation and contrast it with cohesion-tension are demonstrating extended understanding. Students whose REASONING is circular ('it goes up because transpiration makes it go up') need the bridging question: 'What property of water molecules makes the pull of transpiration work all the way down to the roots?'

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the class Driving Question Board, which has been accumulating questions and evidence cards since Lesson 1. Each student is given one sticky note and asked to respond to the specific question: 'Why does the Kibwezi maize wilt even when the farmer has already watered the soil?' Students write their answer in 2–3 sentences, explicitly using the evidence from today's potometer investigation and the cohesion-tension mechanism. Students post their sticky notes on the DQB under the heading 'LESSON 7 ANSWER: Rate of Transpiration vs. Rate of Absorption.' The teacher then facilitates a 5-minute whole-class gallery walk where students read 4–5 sticky notes posted by peers. After the gallery walk, the teacher narrates the current state of the class's collective understanding by pointing to different cards on the DQB, showing how Lessons 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 have progressively built the answer to the driving question. Teacher explicitly names which parts of the driving question REMAIN unanswered (e.g., 'Why do some plants recover by evening but others die permanently?' — pointing forward to Lesson 8).
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Distribute one sticky note per student. Say verbatim: 'You have been investigating this question since Lesson 1 — why does the Kibwezi maize wilt even after watering? Today you have real data. Write your best answer in 2–3 sentences on this sticky note. You must include: (a) the word TRANSPIRATION, (b) the word ABSORPTION, and (c) at least one number from your potometer data.' Give 4 minutes of individual writing time. 2. Say: 'Post your note on the DQB under Lesson 7. Then do a silent gallery walk — read at least 4 notes that are NOT yours. In your notebook, write down one answer that is BETTER or MORE COMPLETE than yours, and explain why.' Give 5 minutes for the gallery walk. 3. Return to the front. Point to the DQB and narrate: 'In Lesson 4, we learned that xylem vessels are the pipelines. In Lesson 5, we learned that osmosis pulls water into the root hair. In Lesson 6, we learned that stomata open and close, controlling water loss. TODAY, we learned that transpiration pull — the cohesion-tension mechanism — is the ENGINE that drives the whole journey.' (Trace the journey on a simple diagram as you speak.) 4. Say: 'But look — we still have unanswered questions on this board.' Point to cards asking: 'Why do SOME plants recover by evening?' and 'What happens to the xylem when the plant permanently wilts?' Say: 'Those questions belong to Lesson 8. Come ready.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB PROGRESSIVE NARRATIVE + GALLERY WALK: The DQB is a public record of the class's growing understanding. By physically pointing to how each lesson has added a piece to the answer, the teacher makes the storyline of the unit visible and coherent. The gallery walk activates social comparison — students evaluate their own explanations against peers' and identify what a stronger explanation looks like, which is both a metacognitive and argumentative practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all sticky notes after class. Observable success indicators: (a) student correctly identifies that transpiration RATE exceeds absorption RATE as the cause of wilting; (b) student references the cohesion-tension mechanism by name; (c) student includes at least one quantitative reference (even approximate). Students who write only 'the plant loses water through leaves' without referencing rates or mechanism need targeted follow-up at the start of Lesson 8. Teacher sorts sticky notes into three piles: Strong (all three criteria met), Developing (two criteria), Needs Support (one or fewer criteria) — these piles inform Lesson 8 grouping decisions.

framework.learnerExperience: Students retrieve their cumulative transport model — a diagram of the full plant (roots, stem, leaves) that they have been adding to since Lesson 4. In this lesson, they make the following additions using a different coloured pen (to show what was added in Lesson 7): (1) Add ARROWS inside the xylem labelled 'cohesion-tension pull' pointing upward from root to leaf; (2) Add a label at the leaf showing 'transpiration — water exits via stomata → creates tension → pulls water column up'; (3) Add a small upward arrow at the root labelled 'root pressure (supplementary)'; (4) Add a new annotation box connected to the stomata: 'At 2 pm in Kibwezi: stomata open → high transpiration rate → tension in xylem increases → if soil water is insufficient, column breaks → wilting.' Students then write a one-sentence MODEL CLAIM at the bottom of their diagram: 'My model now explains wilting because…' Students share their updated model with a partner and use the sentence stem: 'One thing my model still does NOT explain is…' — this unsatisfied question is written on a new sticky note and added to the DQB as a 'bridge to Lesson 8' question.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Say: 'Take out your transport model. You have been building this since Lesson 4. Today we add the ENGINE — the force that makes everything else work.' Give students 2 minutes to look over their existing model before adding anything. 2. Say: 'Use a NEW colour. Add three things: (1) cohesion-tension arrows in the xylem, (2) a transpiration label at the leaf connecting to the tension, (3) a root pressure label at the root. You have 8 minutes.' Circulate and check that arrows are directionally correct (upward) and that labels use the correct vocabulary. 3. After 8 minutes, say: 'Now write your MODEL CLAIM at the bottom: My model now explains wilting because… You have 2 minutes.' 4. Pair-share: Say: 'Show your partner your model. Tell them one thing your model NOW explains that it did NOT explain before Lesson 7. Then tell them one thing your model STILL does NOT explain.' Give 3 minutes. 5. Circulate and listen for unanswered questions. Common responses: 'I don't know why some plants recover by evening', 'I don't know what happens to the xylem when it permanently wilts', 'I don't know how food moves — only water.' Say: 'Write those questions on a sticky note and add them to the DQB — those are exactly the questions Lesson 8 will address.' 6. Close by saying: 'Your model is almost complete. In Lesson 8, you will add the final layer — translocation of food through the phloem — and you will present your full model as your final explanation of the Kibwezi phenomenon.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: CUMULATIVE MODEL REVISION WITH COLOUR-CODED ANNOTATIONS: Using a new colour for each lesson's additions makes conceptual growth visible — students can literally see how their model has evolved across 7 lessons. The 'one thing my model still does NOT explain' prompt is a metacognitive strategy that honours incomplete understanding as a legitimate scientific state and creates forward motivation for Lesson 8. The MODEL CLAIM sentence requires students to synthesise the lesson's key idea into a single explanatory statement, which is a high-order cognitive task.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during model revision and checks: (a) cohesion-tension arrows are pointing upward (not downward); (b) transpiration label is connected to the stomata at the leaf (not at the root); (c) root pressure label is at the root and described as 'supplementary' or 'a push force.' Students who draw arrows in the wrong direction or place transpiration at the root reveal a persistent misconception from prior lessons — teacher provides immediate individual correction using the chain-of-students analogy: 'The pull starts at the TOP of the chain — at the leaf — not at the bottom.' MODEL CLAIM sentences are collected (photographed or submitted) to assess whether students can synthesise the cohesion-tension mechanism into a coherent causal explanation.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) DATA QUALITY: Did all groups obtain a clear, moving bubble in their potometer? If not, what set-up issues arose (leaky connections, no visible bubble, stem blocked) and how will you address these for a repeat or for Lesson 8 review? (2) CER QUALITY: When you review students' written CER explanations, how many correctly linked their numerical data to the cohesion-tension mechanism (not just described the data)? Which students defaulted to textbook language without referencing their own evidence — and what does this tell you about their relationship to inquiry? (3) MISCONCEPTIONS OBSERVED: Did any students persist in believing that roots 'pump' water up (root pressure as the PRIMARY mechanism rather than supplementary)? How did you address this — and do you need to revisit it at the start of Lesson 8? (4) DQB STICKY NOTES: After sorting sticky notes into Strong / Developing / Needs Support, what proportion of the class can correctly articulate that wilting results from transpiration rate exceeding absorption rate? How will you use this information to group students in Lesson 8? (5) PHENOMENON CONNECTION: Did students consistently connect their potometer data back to Kibwezi — or did the investigation feel disconnected from the farmer's problem? If the latter, consider opening Lesson 8 by re-reading the phenomenon aloud and asking students to point to the specific part of their model that now explains each observation (afternoon wilting, recovery by evening, shade protecting the sukuma wiki).

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed an air bubble moving through a calibrated capillary tube in an improvised potometer, measuring how fast a cut leafy stem took up water under four conditions: control (still air), wind (fan/blowing), heat (lamp), and blocked stomata (petroleum jelly). They observed that wind and heat conditions produced the fastest bubble movement (highest water uptake rates), while blocking the stomata dramatically slowed the bubble. Students also observed class-pooled data posted on the board and drew bar graphs comparing uptake rates across all four conditions.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that water moves up the xylem primarily through the cohesion-tension mechanism: transpiration at the leaf surface lowers water potential, creating tension that pulls the cohesive water column upward from roots to leaves. Root pressure provides a supplementary upward push. The rate of water uptake increases when transpiration is accelerated by wind and heat — conditions that match a hot, dry Kibwezi afternoon. Blocking stomata (petroleum jelly) sharply reduces water uptake, confirming that transpiration through stomata is the primary driver of the entire upward journey.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students explained, using the CER framework and their potometer data, that the Kibwezi maize wilts even after watering because the rate of transpiration in the hot, windy afternoon exceeds the rate at which water can be absorbed from the soil and transported up the xylem. When transpiration rate is very high, the tension in the xylem increases; if water in the soil is insufficient or absorption is too slow, the cohesive water column cannot be replenished fast enough, causing cells to lose turgor and the plant to wilt. Students updated their cumulative transport model with cohesion-tension arrows, a transpiration label at the leaf, and a root pressure label at the root.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: cohesion-tension mechanism, transpiration pull, root pressure, water potential, potometer, xylem, wilting, water uptake rate, stomata, semi-arid, Kibwezi, maize, CER framework, bar graph, water column, turgor, evaporation, hydrogen bonds, absorption rate, driving question board

LESSONS.title: The Return Journey: Translocation and Final Model Build

slo.purpose: To consolidate understanding of plant transport by examining phloem translocation through the pressure-flow hypothesis, completing a final annotated whole-plant transport model, and constructing a full evidence-based scientific explanation of the Kibwezi wilting phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) explain the structure and function of phloem in translocating sugars and amino acids from source to sink; (b) describe the pressure-flow hypothesis as the mechanism driving translocation; (c) distinguish between xylem transport (water and mineral salts, root to leaf, transpiration pull) and phloem transport (sucrose and amino acids, source to sink, pressure flow); (d) explain the evidence from girdling experiments that supports phloem function.
slo.skills: The learner should be able to: (a) construct and annotate a complete, cumulative whole-plant transport model integrating osmosis, xylem, phloem, stomatal transpiration, and the pressure-flow hypothesis; (b) analyse photographic evidence of girdled trees to infer the role of phloem; (c) write a structured, evidence-based scientific explanation of the anchoring phenomenon; (d) evaluate and compare their Lesson 1 initial model against their final model to identify conceptual growth.
slo.attitudes: The learner should: (a) appreciate that scientific understanding is built incrementally through evidence gathered across multiple investigations; (b) recognise the real-world relevance of plant transport biology to food security and farming in semi-arid regions like Kibwezi, Makueni County; (c) value collaborative sensemaking and the role of peer critique in refining scientific models.
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants? How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the final lesson in the Plant Transport unit. Students have previously built understanding of osmosis at root hairs (L1–L2), xylem structure and cohesion-tension theory (L3–L4), transpiration and stomatal regulation (L5–L6), and the role of mineral deficiency in plant failure (L7). In this lesson, students complete the 'return journey' — examining how sugars manufactured in leaves travel back down through phloem to roots, fruits, and growing tips. This closes the loop on the Kibwezi phenomenon: students can now explain not only why the maize wilts (water loss exceeds uptake via transpiration pull and xylem) but also why some plants die (phloem failure, sugar starvation of roots, collapse of the whole transport system). The final cumulative model and written explanation represent mastery of all unit learning outcomes.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous chemicals are used in this lesson. If girdling demonstrations use real plant stems, ensure students do not use sharp tools unsupervised. Photographs of girdled mango or eucalyptus trees from Kenyan farms may be used in lieu of live specimens. Ensure all printed materials are returned and recycled responsibly (PCIs: Environmental Conservation).

LESSONS.overview: In this final lesson of Sub-Strand 2.2, students examine photographic evidence of girdled trees — where the bark (phloem) is removed in a ring — and observe that sugars accumulate above the girdle while roots below starve and die. This provides direct empirical evidence for downward phloem transport. Students then apply the pressure-flow (mass flow) hypothesis to explain how a high-pressure source (photosynthesising leaf cells loaded with sucrose) pushes sugars through sieve tubes towards low-pressure sinks (roots, fruits, growing tips). Students use this understanding to complete their final cumulative whole-plant transport model, annotating xylem and phloem pathways, osmosis at root hairs, stomatal transpiration, cohesion-tension, and pressure flow. Groups then present their models and co-construct a full class scientific explanation of the Kibwezi wilting phenomenon — answering every question on the Driving Question Board. The lesson closes with a model comparison (Lesson 1 vs. Lesson 8), a teacher-facilitated DQB review confirming all questions are answered, and individual written Final Explanations submitted as a unit summative task.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown two photographs side-by-side on the board (or printed handouts): (1) a healthy mango tree on a farm near Mombasa, and (2) a mango tree whose bark has been removed in a complete ring around the trunk (girdling) — showing a swollen, bulging ring of tissue above the cut, wilting and dead roots below, and abundant fruit set just above the girdle. Students work individually for 3 minutes to write predictions in their science notebooks: 'What do you think is happening in this tree? Where is water moving? Where is food moving? Will this tree survive?' They then share predictions with a partner (Think-Pair-Share, 2 minutes). The teacher uses cold-calling (4 students) to surface predictions on the board under two columns: 'Water story' and 'Food story.' Students also re-read the Kibwezi scenario card (distributed in Lesson 1) and write one sentence: 'What question about the Kibwezi maize can I now almost answer — and what do I still not know?'
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two mango tree photographs. Say: 'Look carefully at both images. Do not call out yet — I want everyone to think privately first.' [WAIT TIME: 15 seconds]. Say: 'Write your prediction in your notebook. You have 3 minutes. Use these sentence starters: I think water is… I think food is… I think this tree will…' [3 minutes individual writing]. After Think-Pair-Share, cold-call 4 students — two who look confident and two who appear uncertain: 'Amina, tell us your water story.' / 'Omondi, do you agree with Amina or do you have a different idea?' / 'Wanjiru, what does your partner think will happen to the roots?' / 'Kipchoge, what question do you still have?' Record all predictions verbatim on the board under 'Water story' and 'Food story' — do NOT correct errors yet. Say: 'We are going to use today's evidence to test every single one of these predictions. Keep your notebook open — you will be revising these predictions before the lesson ends.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with Cold-Call: This strategy activates prior knowledge from Lessons 1–7 (osmosis, xylem, transpiration) and creates cognitive dissonance — students who understand xylem will predict water moves upward, but the girdling image challenges them to think about a separate downward food-transport pathway. Recording predictions publicly creates accountability and a reference point for revision.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Students write at least two distinct predictions — one about water movement and one about food/sugar movement — showing they are beginning to distinguish between two separate transport systems. Teacher scans notebooks during cold-call and notes any students who conflate xylem and phloem (e.g., 'water carries the food down too') — these students will be targeted for support during Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Activity A — Girdling Evidence Analysis (15 minutes): Students work in groups of 4 to analyse a structured evidence card containing: (1) annotated photographs of a girdled eucalyptus tree from a plantation near Kericho — showing swelling above girdle, dead roots below; (2) a data table showing sucrose concentration above vs. below girdle in a girdled bean plant after 48 hours (above girdle: 4.2%, below girdle: 0.3%); (3) a diagram of phloem sieve tube cells and companion cells. Groups answer guided questions: 'Where did the sucrose accumulate? Why can it not cross the girdle? What does this tell you about which tissue carries sugar? In which direction does sugar normally travel?' Activity B — Pressure-Flow Model Demonstration (10 minutes): The teacher demonstrates the pressure-flow hypothesis using a simple physical model: two beakers connected by a glass tube (representing sieve tubes). Beaker A (source, leaf) contains concentrated sucrose solution dyed red; Beaker B (sink, root) contains dilute sucrose solution. A semi-permeable membrane separates each beaker from a water reservoir. Students observe water entering Beaker A by osmosis (pressure builds), pushing red solution toward Beaker B. Students sketch the model in their notebooks and label: source, sink, high pressure, low pressure, direction of flow, osmosis.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute evidence cards to groups. Say: 'You have 15 minutes. Your group must reach consensus on all four questions — every member must be able to explain the group's answer. I will cold-call one person from each group, and it may not be the person who wrote the answer.' [Circulate, spending 2–3 minutes per group. Listen for: 'the food goes down' / 'the bark carries sugar' / 'the tree starves at the bottom'.] After 15 minutes, cold-call one reporter per group: 'Group 3 — Nyambura, in one sentence, what does the girdling data tell us about which tissue carries sugar?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds before accepting response.] After all groups report, say: 'Let me show you a model of HOW the sugar is pushed.' Conduct the pressure-flow demonstration. Narrate aloud: 'This red solution is sucrose leaving a photosynthesising leaf cell — this is our source. Watch what happens when osmosis builds pressure here.' After the demonstration, say: 'Sketch this in your notebook right now. Label source, sink, and draw an arrow showing direction of flow. You have 90 seconds.' [WAIT TIME: 90 seconds, then cold-call 2 students to share their sketches via document camera or verbal description.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Evidence-Based Reasoning + Physical Analogy Model: The girdling evidence card moves students from prediction to empirical reasoning — they must use concentration data and anatomical diagrams to infer function. The pressure-flow physical demonstration makes an abstract molecular mechanism visible and tangible, directly connecting the chemistry of sucrose concentration gradients to the mechanics of mass flow. Both activities are anchored to the Kibwezi context: if phloem fails (due to drought stress or pest damage to bark), sugars cannot reach roots — the root system weakens, water uptake collapses, and the plant dies rather than recovers.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Group written answers show correct identification of phloem as the sucrose-transporting tissue, correct directionality (source leaf → sink root/fruit), and use of the terms 'concentration gradient' and 'pressure.' Teacher listens during circulation for misconceptions: 'sugar travels in xylem alongside water' (target for Phase 3 direct instruction). Notebook sketches of pressure-flow model should show correctly labelled arrows; teacher checks 4–5 notebooks during the 90-second sketch time.

framework.learnerExperience: Part A — Direct Instruction Consolidation (10 minutes): The teacher delivers a focused, structured explanation of the pressure-flow (mass flow) hypothesis, explicitly connecting each step to the Kibwezi maize plant: (1) Sucrose is produced by photosynthesis in mesophyll cells of the maize leaf (source). (2) Sucrose is actively loaded into phloem sieve tubes — water follows by osmosis, raising hydrostatic pressure. (3) At the sink (root cells, cob, growing tip), sucrose is unloaded and used; water leaves by osmosis, lowering pressure. (4) The pressure gradient drives mass flow from source to sink. Part B — Compare and Contrast (10 minutes): Students complete a structured T-table in their notebooks comparing XYLEM and PHLOEM across six criteria: tissue type, contents transported, direction of transport, driving force, living/non-living cells, evidence used to establish function. Students work individually for 5 minutes, then peer-check with a partner. Part C — Connecting to Kibwezi (5 minutes): Students re-read the Kibwezi scenario. The teacher poses three focus questions written on the board: (1) 'Why does the maize wilt even though soil has water?' (2) 'Why do some plants die while others recover by evening?' (3) 'Why does the sukuma wiki under the mango tree barely wilt?' Students write 2-sentence answers to each question individually, drawing explicitly on evidence from Lessons 1–8.
framework.teacherMoves: For Part A, use the board to draw a maize plant outline — annotate live as you narrate the pressure-flow steps. Say: 'Watch my pen. I am drawing what is happening right now inside a maize plant in Kibwezi.' After each of the 4 steps, pause and ask: 'Can someone predict what happens next?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds, cold-call 1 student.] After Part A, say: 'Now I want you to be the expert. Open your notebook to a blank page. Draw this T-table. You have 5 minutes — silence please, individual work.' [5 minutes.] After peer-check, cold-call 3 students to share one row each of the T-table: 'Chebet, read us your row on driving force.' / 'Mwangi, do you agree? Is there anything to add or correct?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.] Correct any remaining misconception that xylem and phloem carry the same substances in the same direction. For Part C, say: 'These three questions are the heart of our entire unit. Write your best answers now — use specific evidence from our experiments and readings. No copying from a neighbour.' [5 minutes individual writing.] Cold-call 3 students (one per question): 'Akinyi, give us your answer to question 2 — why do some plants die while others recover?' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Note-Making + Phenomenon Reconnection: The T-table forces students to explicitly articulate the distinction between xylem and phloem across multiple dimensions, preventing the persistent misconception that both systems do the same job. Reconnecting all three questions to Kibwezi at the end of Phase 3 ensures that declarative knowledge (pressure-flow mechanism) is immediately linked to explanatory power (why the farmer's maize behaves as it does). This is consistent with NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 6 (Constructing Explanations) and Practice 2 (Developing and Using Models).
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: T-table entries correctly distinguish xylem (water + minerals, dead cells, cohesion-tension, upward) from phloem (sucrose + amino acids, living sieve tubes + companion cells, pressure flow, source-to-sink). Kibwezi written answers should reference specific mechanisms: transpiration exceeding uptake (Q1), stomatal closure and overnight recovery (Q2), reduced transpiration demand in shade (Q3). Teacher collects 6 random notebooks at end of Phase 3 for rapid review before Phase 4.

framework.learnerExperience: The class gathers around the Driving Question Board (physical or digital) that has been built incrementally across Lessons 1–7. The board displays all student-generated questions from the unit, organised under three clusters: (A) Water and mineral uptake, (B) Water movement through the plant, (C) Food transport and plant survival. The teacher reads each question aloud. For each question, the class identifies which lesson(s) provided the answer, which evidence activity (experiment, girdling data, celery staining, etc.) was decisive, and which mechanism explains it. Students physically move question cards from the 'Still wondering' column to the 'We can explain this now' column — or add a written answer sticky note. Any questions that remain unanswered are acknowledged honestly and recorded as 'Questions for further investigation' (connecting to the nature of science). The teacher highlights the journey: 'In Lesson 1 we could not explain why the Kibwezi maize wilted. Look at how many questions we can now answer.' Students spend 3 minutes writing a personal DQB reflection in their notebooks: 'The question I found hardest to answer was… because… Now I understand it because…'
framework.teacherMoves: Stand at the DQB. Say: 'This board has been our scientific record for eight lessons. Let us honour the work by going through every question.' Read each question clearly and slowly. For each: 'Who remembers which lesson helped us answer this? What was the evidence?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds, cold-call different students for each question — aim for at least 12 different students across the full DQB review.] When a question is moved to 'answered,' say: 'What experiment or observation gave us the right to say that?' — insist students cite specific evidence, not just recall vocabulary. If a question is partially answered, say: 'Let us be honest scientists — we can partially explain this. What do we still not know?' Record unanswered questions in a different colour. After the full DQB review, give 3 minutes of silent reflection writing. Cold-call 2 students to share their reflections: 'Ouma, which question was hardest for you? Tell us why.' / 'Zawadi, what changed in your thinking the most?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds each.] Say: 'This is exactly how real scientists work — they build understanding question by question, experiment by experiment. Prof. Wangari Maathai did not understand why deforestation caused soil erosion in one day. It took years of careful observation. You have done something similar in 8 lessons.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Synthesis: The DQB is a visible, cumulative record of the class's epistemic journey. Physically moving questions from 'wondering' to 'explained' externalises metacognition — students see their own intellectual growth as a concrete, spatial record. Requiring students to cite specific evidence for each answer reinforces the norm that scientific claims must be supported by data, not just assertions. The personal reflection writing deepens individual ownership of the learning.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Students can correctly identify the specific lesson, activity, and mechanism that answers each DQB question without prompting. Personal reflection entries show genuine metacognitive awareness — students identify a specific concept (not just 'everything') that was difficult, and articulate specifically what changed their thinking. Teacher listens for unprompted cross-lesson connections (e.g., 'the celery staining in Lesson 3 showed us the xylem path, and today the girdling showed us the phloem path is separate') as evidence of deep integration.

framework.learnerExperience: Part A — Final Model Construction (15 minutes): Each group receives a large blank outline of a maize plant (roots, stem, leaf, cob) on A3 paper. Groups have access to their Lesson 1 initial model for comparison. Using coloured pens (blue for water/xylem, red for sugars/phloem, green for osmosis arrows, orange for transpiration), groups construct their final annotated model. Required annotations: (1) Osmosis at root hair cells — water potential gradient, direction of movement; (2) Xylem vessel pathway — contents (water + mineral salts), driving force (transpiration pull / cohesion-tension), direction (root → leaf); (3) Stomatal transpiration — guard cell mechanism, environmental triggers (heat, wind, drought); (4) Phloem sieve tube pathway — contents (sucrose + amino acids), driving force (pressure-flow / mass flow), direction (source leaf → sink root/cob); (5) Kibwezi wilting annotation — mark exactly where the transport system breaks down in the wilting scenario and why. Part B — Group Presentations (10 minutes): Each group presents their final model (2 minutes per group, 4 groups). One designated 'challenger' from another group asks one question. Part C — Individual Written Final Explanation (10 minutes): Working individually and in silence, each student writes a structured scientific explanation (minimum 150 words) answering the full driving question: 'How does water and food travel through an entire plant — from soil to the highest leaf — and why does this journey sometimes fail, causing crops like maize in Kibwezi to wilt and die?' The explanation must reference: osmosis, water potential, xylem, cohesion-tension, transpiration, stomata, phloem, pressure-flow hypothesis, source, sink, and at least one specific environmental factor from Kibwezi (heat, drought, semi-arid conditions). Part D — Model Comparison (3 minutes): Students place their Lesson 1 model beside their Lesson 8 model. They write one sentence: 'My biggest scientific growth in this unit was…' These are collected as evidence of learning progression.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute A3 maize outlines and coloured pens. Return Lesson 1 models to each group. Say: 'You have 15 minutes. Your final model must tell the complete story — from a raindrop soaking into the soil in Kibwezi, to that water evaporating from a stomata on the top leaf, AND the journey of a sucrose molecule from a leaf mesophyll cell down to a root tip. Every arrow must be labelled. Every colour must be used correctly.' [Circulate, spending 2–3 minutes per group. Prompt with: 'Where does the pressure-flow begin in this plant?' / 'Show me exactly where the transport fails when the maize wilts.' / 'Is that arrow pointing the right direction for phloem?'] During presentations, instruct challengers: 'Your question must start with: How does your model explain…' After presentations, say: 'Now close your group work. Individual silence. This final explanation is yours alone.' [10 minutes writing.] At the 5-minute mark, say quietly: 'Make sure you have named at least one specific environmental condition in Kibwezi that disrupts transport.' After writing, say: 'Place your Lesson 1 model beside your Lesson 8 model. Look at both. Write one sentence beginning: My biggest scientific growth in this unit was…' Collect all final explanations and model comparison sentences. Close the lesson by reading the driving question aloud one final time and saying: 'Every word of that question — you can now answer. That is biology.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision + Evidence-Based Scientific Explanation Writing: The final model build is the capstone sensemaking activity of the entire unit. Requiring colour-coded annotation of both xylem and phloem on the same plant diagram forces students to integrate two distinct transport systems into a unified whole — they cannot treat them separately. The Kibwezi wilting annotation specifically requires students to apply their model to the original phenomenon, demonstrating transfer. The written Final Explanation (NGSS SEP 6: Constructing Explanations) requires students to synthesise mechanism, evidence, and context into a coherent scientific argument — this is the highest cognitive demand of the unit. The model comparison (L1 vs. L8) makes learning progression visible and supports metacognitive reflection, a key competency in Kenya's CBC framework.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators — Final model: All five required annotations are present and correctly labelled; colour coding is consistent; Kibwezi wilting annotation correctly identifies the breakdown point (transpiration rate exceeds xylem supply rate; and/or phloem failure starves roots; and/or stomata fail to close due to loss of guard cell turgor). Written Final Explanation: Minimum 150 words; all 10 required terms used correctly in context; Kibwezi environmental factor named and mechanistically linked to transport failure. Model comparison sentence: Identifies a specific conceptual shift (not generic), demonstrating genuine metacognitive awareness. Teacher uses final explanations as summative assessment evidence for the unit.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After delivering this lesson, reflect on the following questions:

1. CONCEPTUAL MASTERY: Did students correctly distinguish between xylem and phloem transport in their final models — including directionality, driving forces, and contents? Were there persistent confusions (e.g., phloem moving upward only, or water travelling in phloem) that indicate a need to revisit in Grade 11?

2. PHENOMENON CLOSURE: Were students able to articulate a complete, mechanistically accurate explanation of the Kibwezi wilting scenario in their Final Explanations? Could they explain all three elements: (a) why wilting occurs despite watered soil, (b) why some plants recover by evening while others die, and (c) why the shaded sukuma wiki is protected? If students struggled with (b) or (c), consider adding a dedicated lesson on stomatal regulation and shade effects in future iterations.

3. MODEL QUALITY: Comparing Lesson 1 models to Lesson 8 models — was the growth visible and significant? Were students able to articulate their own growth in the model comparison sentence? If Lesson 1 models were very sparse, consider adding a 'checkpoint model' at Lesson 4 to make progression more gradual and visible.

4. DQB COMPLETENESS: Were all student-generated questions on the DQB answered by the end of this lesson? If questions remained unanswered, note them here and consider whether they should be addressed in Sub-Strand 2.3 (Gaseous Exchange and Respiration) or flagged as genuine open scientific questions.

5. DIFFERENTIATION: Were all learners able to engage with the pressure-flow hypothesis, or did some students remain at a surface-level 'sugars go down' understanding? Consider whether a visual animation or a further guided reading on phloem loading (active transport of sucrose into sieve tubes) would support struggling learners.

6. TIME MANAGEMENT: This lesson is content-rich. If time was short, which phase was compressed? The written Final Explanation (Phase 5C) should never be sacrificed — if time pressure is acute, shorten group presentations (Phase 5B) to 1 minute per group.

7. KENYA CONTEXT: Did students connect the biology to real farming decisions in semi-arid regions like Kibwezi or the wider Makueni County? If not, consider inviting a guest speaker (local agricultural extension officer) or assigning a follow-up task: 'Write a letter to the Kibwezi farmer explaining what is happening to her maize and what she could do differently, using biology.'

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed annotated photographs and concentration data from girdling experiments (eucalyptus trees near Kericho; bean plant sucrose data) showing accumulation of sugars above the girdle and starvation below, providing direct evidence that phloem carries sucrose downward from source to sink. Students also observed a physical pressure-flow demonstration using two beakers, concentrated and dilute sucrose solutions, semi-permeable membranes, and water — watching mass flow driven by osmotic pressure gradients.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that phloem sieve tubes, assisted by companion cells, transport sucrose and amino acids from photosynthetic source cells (leaves) to metabolic sink cells (roots, fruits, growing tips) by mass flow driven by a hydrostatic pressure gradient — the pressure-flow (mass flow) hypothesis. Students consolidated the full plant transport system: osmosis drives water into root hairs down a water potential gradient → cohesion-tension pulls water up xylem → transpiration at stomata drives the pull → sucrose is loaded into phloem at source → pressure-flow moves sucrose to sinks → osmosis unloads water at sink. Students applied this integrated understanding to construct a complete annotated model and to explain why the Kibwezi maize wilts, why some plants die, and why shaded sukuma wiki is protected.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students explained the anchoring phenomenon — 'The Wilting Maize of Kibwezi' — in full: (1) Despite watered soil, the rate of transpiration through stomata on a hot, dry Kibwezi afternoon exceeds the rate of water uptake and xylem transport, creating a water deficit in leaf cells, causing loss of turgor and wilting. (2) Plants near the dry seasonal riverbed wilt sooner because soil water potential is lower and root uptake is less efficient, meaning the xylem system cannot replace transpired water fast enough; if guard cells lose turgor and fail to close stomata, or if the phloem system is compromised by drought stress, the plant cannot recover and dies. (3) Sukuma wiki under the mango tree experiences lower transpiration demand because shade reduces leaf temperature and the vapour pressure gradient between leaf and air — stomata need not open as wide, less water is lost, the xylem supply is sufficient, turgor is maintained, and the plant does not wilt.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: plant transport, phloem, xylem, translocation, pressure-flow hypothesis, mass flow, source to sink, sucrose transport, girdling experiment, sieve tubes, companion cells, cohesion-tension, transpiration pull, stomata, osmosis, root hair cells, water potential, turgor pressure, wilting, Kibwezi, maize, sukuma wiki, semi-arid farming, final model build, driving question board, scientific explanation, Kenya Grade 10 Biology, CBE, KICD, Sub-Strand 2.2

FINAL_EXPLANATION.instructions: This is your Final Explanation — your chance to show everything you have learned across all 8 lessons about plant transport. Your goal is to answer the driving question completely and confidently, using scientific evidence and the correct vocabulary you have built up. Read each section prompt carefully, then write your response in the space provided. Use the exemplars as a guide for the level of detail expected — but write in your own words. Remember: a strong Final Explanation always connects back to the farmer in Kibwezi. Her maize is your anchor. Explain the science so well that she would understand exactly why her crop is wilting — and what is happening inside every plant on her farm.

sections.title: Section 1 — The Journey of Water: From Kibwezi Soil to the Highest Maize Leaf
sections.prompt: Describe the complete pathway that a water molecule takes from the soil outside a maize root all the way to the air above the highest leaf. In your answer: (a) name each tissue and structure the water passes through in order, (b) explain the driving force that pulls water upward at each stage, and (c) explain why the distance to the water table matters for the maize plants growing near the dry riverbed in Kibwezi.
sections.exemplar:
A water molecule in the Kibwezi soil first enters the maize plant through root hair cells by osmosis. Root hair cells have a lower water potential than the surrounding soil solution (when soil moisture is adequate), so water moves down a water potential gradient into the cell. The water then moves through the root cortex — either through the cytoplasm of cells (symplast pathway) or through the cell walls (apoplast pathway) — until it reaches the endodermis. The Casparian strip in the endodermis blocks the apoplast route and forces all water into the symplast, acting as a checkpoint that controls which minerals enter the xylem. Water then passes into the xylem vessels in the root.

Once inside the xylem, water is pulled upward through the stem and into the leaves by a process called transpiration pull (cohesion-tension mechanism). As water evaporates from the mesophyll cells inside the leaf through the stomata — a process called transpiration — it creates tension (negative pressure) in the xylem. Because water molecules are strongly attracted to each other (cohesion) and to the walls of the xylem vessels (adhesion), this tension pulls the entire water column upward continuously, like water rising through a very thin straw. This is called the cohesion-tension theory. Root pressure also contributes a small upward push, especially at night when transpiration is low.

Finally, water reaches the leaf mesophyll cells and either stays to be used in photosynthesis or evaporates through the stomata into the atmosphere.

The maize plants near the dry seasonal riverbed in Kibwezi wilt sooner because the water table there is much deeper. The xylem water column must span a greater height, and if the soil around the upper roots dries out, the water column can break (cavitation), and the cohesion-tension mechanism fails. There is simply not enough water close to the roots to maintain continuous flow, so the plant wilts even if some moisture remains deeper in the soil.

sections.title: Section 2 — Transpiration: Why the Maize Loses Water Faster Than It Can Absorb It
sections.prompt: Explain what transpiration is and why it happens. Then use your understanding of stomata, guard cells, and environmental conditions to explain: (a) why the Kibwezi maize wilts specifically in the afternoon rather than in the morning, and (b) why the sukuma wiki growing in the shade of the mango tree barely wilts at all. Include at least TWO environmental factors in your answer.
sections.exemplar:
Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from a plant, mainly through tiny pores on the leaf surface called stomata. It happens because the air spaces inside the leaf are humid (high water potential) while the outside air is drier (lower water potential), so water vapour moves down this gradient and diffuses out. Transpiration is not a mistake — it is an unavoidable consequence of leaves opening their stomata to let in carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.

Stomata are opened and closed by pairs of guard cells. When guard cells absorb water by osmosis, they become turgid and bow outward, opening the pore. When they lose water, they become flaccid and the pore closes. Guard cells respond to light, carbon dioxide concentration, and water availability.

The Kibwezi maize wilts in the afternoon — not the morning — because transpiration rate is highest in the afternoon. By midday and early afternoon, temperatures are at their peak (Kibwezi is semi-arid and can be very hot), which increases the kinetic energy of water molecules, speeding up evaporation. High temperature also reduces air humidity, steepening the water potential gradient between the leaf interior and the atmosphere. Wind speed may also be higher, carrying away humid air from around the leaf surface and further increasing water loss. All of these factors make the rate of transpiration far exceed the rate of water absorption by the roots, so the plant cells lose turgor and wilt.

The sukuma wiki under the mango tree barely wilts because shade reduces two key drivers of transpiration: (1) Light intensity is lower, so guard cells receive less light stimulus and stomata remain more closed, reducing water vapour loss. (2) Temperature under the tree canopy is cooler, slowing evaporation. This means the sukuma wiki loses water much more slowly, and the rate of absorption through the roots keeps pace with the rate of loss — so the plant stays turgid and upright.

sections.title: Section 3 — Translocation: Moving Food from Leaves to the Rest of the Plant
sections.prompt: The farmer in Kibwezi notices that even when her maize plants look healthy and green, the cobs (maize ears) sometimes fail to fill properly during a drought. Explain: (a) how the food (sugars) made in the leaves travels to the developing cob, naming the tissue and process involved, (b) the source-to-sink model, and (c) how severe water stress can disrupt translocation and affect grain filling in maize.
sections.exemplar:
While water travels upward through xylem, the sugars produced by photosynthesis in the leaves travel through a different tissue called the phloem. The movement of dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) and other organic compounds through the phloem is called translocation. Unlike xylem transport, which is passive and driven by physical forces, translocation requires energy (ATP) at key loading and unloading steps.

The process is explained by the pressure-flow (mass flow) hypothesis. Leaves are called the source — they actively load sucrose into the phloem sieve tubes using companion cells and ATP-powered carrier proteins. This increases the solute concentration inside the phloem at the source, so water enters the phloem by osmosis from nearby xylem, creating high hydrostatic pressure. At the other end, the developing maize cob (and roots) are called sinks — they unload sucrose for growth and energy, reducing solute concentration. Water leaves the phloem at the sink by osmosis, lowering pressure. The pressure difference between source and sink drives a continuous mass flow of phloem sap from source to sink.

During severe drought in Kibwezi, water stress disrupts translocation in two ways. First, if leaves lose too much water, photosynthesis slows or stops — so less sugar is produced at the source, reducing the pressure gradient. Second, the companion cells that actively load sucrose into the phloem require ATP, and a severely stressed plant redirects resources away from growth. The result is that the developing maize cob (sink) receives insufficient sucrose for grain filling, producing small, poorly developed cobs — even on plants that appeared outwardly healthy earlier in the season. This is a hidden cost of drought that the farmer may not connect directly to water stress.

sections.title: Section 4 — Why Some Plants Recover and Others Die: Turgor, Permanent Wilting, and Adaptation
sections.prompt: The farmer observes that by evening, some wilted maize plants recover, while others do not. Using your knowledge of turgor pressure, the permanent wilting point, and plant adaptations, explain: (a) the difference between temporary wilting and permanent wilting, (b) the cellular reason why a plant that passes the permanent wilting point cannot recover, and (c) at least TWO structural or behavioural adaptations that help plants in semi-arid areas like Kibwezi reduce water loss.
sections.exemplar:
Plant cells stay firm and upright because of turgor pressure — the pressure of water pushing outward against the cell wall when a cell is full of water (turgid). When a plant loses water faster than it absorbs it, cells lose turgor and become flaccid, causing the plant to wilt. This is called temporary wilting if the soil still contains water above the permanent wilting point. In temporary wilting, as temperatures drop in the evening, transpiration slows and the plant can reabsorb enough water from the soil to regain turgidity — which is why some of the Kibwezi maize recovers overnight.

However, if the soil water potential drops so low that the roots can no longer absorb water by osmosis — even with stomata fully closed — the plant has reached its permanent wilting point. At this stage, cells have been plasmolysed (the cell membrane has pulled away from the cell wall due to excessive water loss). The normal osmotic gradient needed to pull water in no longer exists, and cellular machinery begins to break down. Even if you water the soil at this point, the damage to membranes, enzymes, and transport proteins may be irreversible. This is why some of the farmer's maize plants do not recover — they passed the permanent wilting point during the hottest part of the afternoon.

Plants adapted to semi-arid conditions like Kibwezi have evolved several features to delay reaching this point: (1) Sunken stomata — some drought-adapted plants have stomata in pits recessed below the leaf surface, which traps humid air and reduces the water potential gradient, slowing transpiration. (2) Thick, waxy cuticle — a thick waxy layer on the leaf surface (as seen in succulent plants) prevents water from evaporating through the leaf surface directly, reducing cuticular transpiration. (3) Stomatal closure during heat — CAM plants open stomata only at night, fixing CO₂ when temperatures are cool and transpiration losses are minimal. (4) Deep or wide root systems — roots that spread broadly or reach deeper soil layers access more water, maintaining the water column even during dry spells.

sections.title: Section 5 — Answering the Driving Question: A Complete Explanation for the Kibwezi Farmer
sections.prompt: You are writing a letter to the maize farmer in Kibwezi. Using everything you have learned across all 8 lessons, write a full scientific explanation that answers the driving question: How does water and food travel through an entire plant — from the soil to the highest leaf — and why does this journey sometimes fail, causing crops like maize in Kibwezi to wilt and die? Your explanation must: (a) describe both xylem (water) and phloem (food) transport, (b) explain the specific reasons her maize wilts in the afternoon, (c) explain why some plants recover and others die, (d) explain why the shaded sukuma wiki survives better, and (e) suggest ONE practical action she could take, based on the science, to protect her maize during the dry season.
sections.exemplar:
Dear Farmer,

Thank you for sharing your observations — they reveal something fascinating about how plants work, and understanding the science behind what you have seen can help you protect your harvest.

Inside every maize plant on your farm, there is an incredible transport system made of two types of tubes running from roots to leaves. The first is the xylem, which carries water and dissolved mineral salts upward from the soil. Water enters through the root hair cells by osmosis — moving from the wet soil into the root because the root cells have a lower water potential. It then travels up through the xylem vessels in the stem, pulled upward by a force called transpiration pull. As water evaporates from the leaves through tiny pores called stomata, it creates a tension that draws more water up from the roots — a bit like drinking through a long straw. The second transport tissue is the phloem, which carries the sugars made by photosynthesis in the leaves downward (and upward) to wherever the plant needs energy — including the developing maize cob. This process is called translocation and is driven by pressure differences between sugar-producing leaves (sources) and sugar-using organs like the cob (sinks).

Now, why does your maize wilt in the afternoon? By midday in Kibwezi, the temperature is very high and the air is dry. These conditions cause your maize leaves to lose water through the stomata far faster than the roots can absorb it from the soil. Even if you watered the soil that morning, the plant simply cannot move water upward quickly enough to replace what is being lost. The cells lose their water pressure (turgor) and the plant droops. This is not a failure of your watering — it is the physics of water movement struggling to keep up with the heat of a semi-arid afternoon.

The plants near the dry riverbed wilt sooner because the soil there dries out faster and the water table is deeper. The xylem water column breaks more easily with less water in the soil, stopping transport altogether.

Why do some plants recover in the evening but others do not? As the sun sets and temperatures fall, transpiration slows. Plants that still have enough soil water can reabsorb it and regain their turgor — they were only temporarily wilted. But plants that lost too much water reach what scientists call the permanent wilting point: the soil is so dry that the roots cannot pull water in even when the stomata are closed. These plants suffer cell damage and cannot recover, which is why some of your maize does not revive overnight.

Your neighbour's sukuma wiki survives better under the mango tree because shade reduces two things: light intensity (so the stomata open less and lose less water) and temperature (so evaporation is slower). The rate of water loss matches the rate of water supply, and the plants stay turgid all day.

One practical step you could take based on this science: apply a layer of mulch (such as dry maize stalks or grass) around the base of your maize plants. Mulch reduces soil surface temperature, slows evaporation from the soil, and helps the soil retain the water you apply in the morning for longer. This keeps water available to the roots during the hottest afternoon hours, helping to delay wilting — and giving more of your plants a chance to recover each evening.

Your observations were not just puzzling — they were science. I hope this explanation helps.

Yours in biology,
[Student Name]

rubric.excellent: Accurately and fully explains both xylem (cohesion-tension, osmosis, root pressure, Casparian strip) and phloem (pressure-flow/mass flow, source-sink model, active loading) transport using correct terminology throughout. No misconceptions present.
rubric.proficient: Correctly explains both xylem and phloem transport with mostly accurate terminology. Minor omissions (e.g., Casparian strip or active loading not mentioned) but no significant misconceptions.
rubric.developing: Attempts to describe transport in xylem and/or phloem but contains one or more misconceptions (e.g., confuses xylem and phloem, states that plants 'suck up' water without explaining the mechanism, or omits the role of osmosis at the root).

rubric.criterion: Explanation of Wilting, Recovery, and Permanent Wilting Point
rubric.excellent: Clearly distinguishes temporary from permanent wilting with reference to turgor pressure, plasmolysis, and the permanent wilting point. Accurately explains at the cellular level why some Kibwezi maize recovers in the evening and others do not, linking soil water potential to root absorption capacity.
rubric.proficient: Correctly explains the difference between temporary and permanent wilting and connects it to the Kibwezi scenario. Cellular-level explanation (e.g., plasmolysis) may be incomplete but the overall reasoning is sound.
rubric.developing: Recognises that some plants wilt permanently but cannot explain why at the cellular or tissue level. May simply state 'the plant ran out of water' without reference to turgor pressure, water potential, or the permanent wilting point.

rubric.criterion: Application to the Anchoring Phenomenon (Kibwezi Maize)
rubric.excellent: Every section is explicitly and meaningfully connected to the Kibwezi farmer's observations (afternoon wilting, riverbed vs. slope location, shaded sukuma wiki). Scientific concepts are used to explain each specific observation, not just described in general terms.
rubric.proficient: Most sections connect back to the Kibwezi phenomenon with reasonable specificity. At least three of the farmer's specific observations (afternoon timing, location difference, shade effect) are explained using scientific reasoning.
rubric.developing: Some reference to Kibwezi is made but the connections are superficial or generic (e.g., 'the plant wilts because it is hot in Kibwezi') without applying the underlying transport mechanisms to explain the specific observations.

rubric.excellent: Consistently and correctly uses all key terms: xylem, phloem, transpiration, translocation, osmosis, water potential, turgor pressure, cohesion-tension, stomata, guard cells, source, sink, permanent wilting point, plasmolysis, and Casparian strip. Terms are used in context, not just listed.
rubric.proficient: Uses most key terms correctly in context. May omit 2–3 terms (e.g., plasmolysis or Casparian strip) but demonstrates clear understanding of the core vocabulary: xylem, phloem, transpiration, translocation, osmosis, turgor pressure, stomata, and guard cells.
rubric.developing: Uses fewer than half of the expected key terms, or uses several terms incorrectly. Explanations rely heavily on everyday language (e.g., 'tubes carry water up,' 'leaves breathe') rather than scientific vocabulary.

rubric.criterion: Quality of Scientific Reasoning and Evidence-Based Argument
rubric.excellent: Explanations follow a clear cause-and-effect chain throughout. Claims are supported by mechanisms (not just observations). The final section (letter to farmer) synthesises all concepts into a coherent, complete answer to the driving question and includes a scientifically justified practical recommendation.
rubric.proficient: Reasoning is mostly logical and cause-and-effect relationships are evident in most sections. The final letter addresses the driving question adequately and includes a practical recommendation, though the justification may lack full scientific depth.
rubric.developing: Explanations are descriptive rather than mechanistic — the student describes what happens but not why or how. The final letter may restate observations without providing a scientific explanation, or the practical recommendation is absent or not grounded in the science learned.

SUMMARY_TABLE.drivingQuestion: DRIVING QUESTION: How does water and food travel through an entire plant — from the soil to the highest leaf — and why does this journey sometimes fail, causing crops like maize in Kibwezi to wilt and die?

lessons.title: Anchoring Phenomenon — Wilting Maize in Kibwezi
lessons.observed: Students observed photographs and/or a short video of maize crops in Kibwezi wilting during a dry spell, despite green leaves and apparently healthy stems. They also observed a celery stalk (or white carnation) placed in coloured water and noted that colour travelled upward through the stem over time. Teacher should cold-call at least 4 students to describe what they see before offering any vocabulary — this protects initial observations from teacher bias.
lessons.learned: Students learned that a living plant must continuously move materials — water, minerals, and sugars — from where those materials enter or are made to where they are needed. The wilting maize phenomenon establishes that this transport system can FAIL, and that failure has real consequences for food security in semi-arid regions like Kibwezi, Makueni County. Students also learned the meaning of 'anchoring phenomenon' as the central mystery the class will keep returning to across all 8 lessons.
lessons.explained: Students explained (in initial, rough terms) that 'something must be broken or blocked' when maize wilts — the water that enters the roots is not reaching the leaves. Teacher should record all student ideas on the Driving Question Board (DQB) without correcting them at this stage; misconceptions are valuable diagnostic data. Pedagogical note: preserve student language exactly on the DQB — do NOT rephrase into scientific vocabulary yet. This baseline record will be revisited and revised in Lesson 8.

lessons.title: What Do Plants Need to Survive? — Mapping the Case for a Transport System
lessons.observed: Students examined diagrams and data cards showing where key plant resources enter the plant (water and minerals → roots; carbon dioxide → leaves; light → leaves) and where those resources are consumed (water used in photosynthesis at leaves; minerals used for growth at shoot tips; sugars produced at leaves but needed at roots). Students mapped these mismatches on a large class diagram of a maize plant, using arrows to show the 'resource gap' that transport must bridge.
lessons.learned: Students learned that because plant resources enter at one location but are needed and used at a different location (e.g., water enters at the root but is needed at the leaf for photosynthesis), a specialised internal transport system is essential. Without transport, cells far from the resource entry point would starve or dehydrate. This is why a single-celled organism like Euglena does not need a transport system, but a tall maize plant in Kibwezi does.
lessons.explained: Students explained the 'resource mismatch' problem using their annotated maize diagrams, linking it back to the driving question: if the transport system fails to bridge the gap between roots (water entry) and leaves (water use), the maize wilts. Teacher should use a think-pair-share with the prompt: 'Why can a tiny water plant like Elodea survive without transport tubes, but a maize plant cannot?' Allow 60 seconds of silent thinking before pairs discuss. Cold-call 3 pairs to share reasoning.

lessons.title: The Big Picture: Overview of the Plant Transport System (Xylem and Phloem)
lessons.observed: Students examined cross-section slides (or high-quality photomicrograph cards) of a maize stem, identifying two distinct tissue types within vascular bundles. They also watched a short animation showing water molecules moving upward through xylem vessels and sucrose moving bidirectionally through phloem. Teacher should pause the animation at key moments and ask: 'What direction is this molecule moving — and why might that direction matter for the Kibwezi maize?'
lessons.learned: Students learned that plants have two specialised vascular transport tissues: (1) Xylem — transports water and dissolved mineral salts from roots upward to leaves; made of dead, hollow, lignified cells; and (2) Phloem — transports dissolved organic solutes (sucrose and amino acids) from leaves to all other parts of the plant; made of living sieve tube cells and companion cells. These two tissues together form the vascular bundle, visible in both stem and leaf.
lessons.explained: Students explained the functional difference between xylem and phloem using a two-column comparison table and annotated the cross-section diagram with both tissue labels. They then linked this overview to the driving question: wilting in Kibwezi maize could involve failure in xylem (water not reaching leaves), phloem (sugars not reaching roots), or both. Pedagogical note: insist students use the word 'because' in every explanation — this forces causal reasoning rather than simple labelling.

lessons.title: The Entry Point: Root Structure and Water Absorption
lessons.observed: Students observed a demonstration or simulation of osmosis using visking tubing (or a raw potato strip) placed in solutions of different concentrations, recording changes in mass or volume over 15 minutes. They also examined diagrams of root hair cells, noting the large surface area created by the elongated hair-like projection. Teacher should ask: 'What does the shape of a root hair cell tell you about its function?' — wait 30 seconds before taking responses.
lessons.learned: Students learned that water enters the root by osmosis — moving passively from soil water (high water potential) through root hair cells, across the cortex, through the endodermis, and into the xylem. Root hair cells are adapted for absorption by their large surface area, thin cell wall, and presence of a large central vacuole. The Casparian strip in the endodermis forces water through cell cytoplasm (symplast pathway), allowing selective control of mineral uptake into the xylem.
lessons.explained: Students explained the step-by-step journey of a single water molecule from soil to xylem vessel, using a flow diagram. They connected this to the Kibwezi context: during drought, soil water potential drops — the osmotic gradient between soil and root hair cells decreases or even reverses, so water absorption by osmosis slows or stops, explaining why maize wilts even before all soil water is gone. Pedagogical note: many students confuse osmosis with active transport here — address this explicitly using the 'no energy needed' rule for osmosis.

lessons.title: The Highway: Stem Structure and Vascular Bundles
lessons.observed: Students dissected a fresh maize stem (or bean stem) cross-section and used a hand lens or low-power microscope to locate vascular bundles. They compared the arrangement of vascular bundles in a monocot stem (maize — scattered) versus a dicot stem (bean — ring arrangement), recording observations as labelled drawings. Teacher should circulate and ask each group: 'Which tissue is closer to the inside of the bundle — and what structural feature helps you tell xylem from phloem?'
lessons.learned: The stem contains vascular bundles made up of two distinct tissues: xylem (inner, thick-walled, dead cells forming hollow tubes for carrying water and minerals upward) and phloem (outer, living sieve tubes for carrying dissolved sugars). Lignin in xylem cell walls provides mechanical support, helping the maize stem stand upright. In a monocot like maize, vascular bundles are scattered throughout the stem; in dicots like beans, they form a ring. The stem acts as the 'highway' connecting the absorbing roots to the photosynthesising leaves.
lessons.explained: Students explained how the structural features of xylem vessels — dead cells, no cross-walls, lignified walls — make them ideal hollow pipes for carrying water under tension without collapsing. They linked stem vascular anatomy to the driving question: if xylem vessels become blocked (e.g., by air bubbles during drought, or by fungal pathogens like Fusarium common in Kenyan maize fields), water flow to leaves is interrupted and wilting follows. Pedagogical note: the 'dead cells = hollow tube' logic is a classic exam point — reinforce with at least one worked exam-style question in class.

lessons.title: The Destination: Leaf Structure and Transpiration
lessons.observed: Students conducted the cobalt chloride paper experiment (or Vaseline leaf experiment) to detect and compare rates of water loss from the upper and lower surfaces of a maize or bean leaf. They recorded colour-change times as quantitative data and calculated which surface lost more water. Teacher should prompt: 'Before you start, predict — which surface will change the cobalt chloride paper faster, and why?' Record class predictions publicly before data collection begins.
lessons.learned: Students learned that transpiration is the loss of water vapour from a plant, mainly through stomata located predominantly in the lower epidermis of leaves. The rate of transpiration is affected by temperature, humidity, wind speed, and light intensity — all factors highly variable in Kenya's semi-arid zones like Kibwezi. Guard cells control stomatal opening and closing: when turgid (water-filled), guard cells bow apart and open the stoma; when flaccid (water-deficient), they close, reducing water loss but also limiting CO₂ entry for photosynthesis.
lessons.explained: Students explained the adaptive trade-off of stomata: open stomata allow CO₂ in for photosynthesis but also allow water vapour to escape. During the dry season in Kibwezi, maize guard cells close stomata to conserve water, but this halts photosynthesis, starving the plant of sugars — a double failure. Students drew and annotated stomata in open and closed states, labelling guard cells, chloroplasts, and the stomatal pore. Pedagogical note: use a local weather data set from Kibwezi (Kenya Meteorological Department records) to make transpiration rate calculations authentic and relevant.

lessons.title: The Engine: Transpiration Pull, Cohesion-Tension, and Root Pressure
lessons.observed: Students observed a potometer experiment (or teacher-led demonstration using a leafy shoot and capillary tube) measuring the rate of water uptake under different conditions (fan on vs. fan off; light vs. dark; humid vs. dry). They plotted results as a bar chart and identified which variable caused the greatest change in uptake rate. Teacher should ask: 'If the shoot takes up more water when the fan is on, what does that tell us about what is PULLING the water up?'
lessons.learned: Students learned that water moves up the xylem primarily through the cohesion-tension mechanism: (1) transpiration at the leaf surface removes water molecules, lowering water potential in leaf cells; (2) water is pulled up the xylem by tension (negative pressure) created by this evaporation; (3) water molecules stick together (cohesion) and to xylem walls (adhesion), forming an unbroken column from root to leaf. Root pressure (generated by active ion uptake creating osmotic pull) provides a supplementary push, especially at night. Together these forces explain how water can rise 30 m up a tall tree — or travel up a 2-metre maize stem in Kibwezi.
lessons.explained: Students explained why breaking the water column — through drought-induced cavitation (air bubbles entering xylem when water is under extreme tension) — causes irreversible wilting in Kibwezi maize. Once the column breaks, transpiration pull cannot operate and water ceases to rise. Students constructed a force-diagram showing the relative contributions of transpiration pull vs. root pressure at different times of day. Pedagogical note: the concept of 'negative pressure pulling water' is deeply counterintuitive — use the analogy of a drinking straw to build intuition before introducing formal terminology.

lessons.title: The Return Journey: Translocation and Final Model Build
lessons.observed: Students examined aphid stylet experiments (diagrams/data) showing phloem sap composition and pressure, and reviewed autoradiograph images demonstrating that radioactively labelled sucrose moves from leaves to roots and to growing shoot tips. They also examined data showing that phloem transport is bidirectional — sucrose moves from any 'source' (leaf) to any 'sink' (root, fruit, young leaf). Teacher should ask: 'How is the direction of phloem transport different from xylem transport — and why does that difference matter?'
lessons.learned: Students learned that phloem sieve tubes, assisted by companion cells, transport sucrose and amino acids from photosynthetic source cells (leaves) to all metabolically active or storage sink cells (roots, fruits, seeds, young leaves) by the pressure-flow (mass flow) mechanism. High sucrose concentration at the source lowers water potential, drawing water in from adjacent xylem and raising hydrostatic pressure; at the sink, sucrose is unloaded, water potential rises, water leaves phloem to xylem, and pressure drops — creating a pressure gradient that drives bulk flow from source to sink.
lessons.explained: Students explained how, during drought in Kibwezi, reduced photosynthesis (because stomata close to conserve water) means less sucrose is loaded into phloem at source cells — so translocation slows, roots and developing maize cobs are starved of sugars, growth stops, and cobs fail to fill. Students then completed their FINAL cumulative model of the maize plant transport system, annotating the full water pathway (soil → root hair → cortex → xylem → stem → leaf mesophyll → stomata → atmosphere) and the full sucrose pathway (leaf mesophyll → phloem → stem → root/cob). Pedagogical note: final models should be compared with the rough initial models from Lesson 1 so students can explicitly articulate what they now understand that they did not before — this metacognitive step is a KICD CBE core competency requirement.
